EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low from central Missouri aided by a surface low over the Missouri Bootheel pinched off a warm sector ahead of a cold front. This warm sector provided enough instability and weak shear to produce a few strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. Sub-severe hail and winds accompanied a few severe hail storms along the Tennessee and  Mississippi border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub-severe hail reported near Savannah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to improve ongoing drought conditions from November 2023 to now with abundant rainfall occurring in December and January. ||While no changes were made from the December 28th issuance for the first Drought Monitor issuance of 2024 on January 4th, rainfall across the area for the first half of January benefitted drought-stricken regions. No changes were made from the issuance on the 4th or 11th in Southern Middle TN. ||Much more improvement was noted by the issuance on the 18th, when Extreme Drought (D3) Conditions were removed from all 3 Southern Middle TN Counties of Lincoln, Franklin, and Moore. Severe Drought (D2) Conditions accounted for the vast majority of the Drought Declarations in Southern Middle TN at this time, with Moderate Drought (D1) Conditions expanding to the NW across portions of Franklin, Lincoln, and Moore Counties. ||Further improvement was noted by the issuance on the 25th, where conditions were degraded down to Abnormally Dry (D0) across portions Franklin and Lincoln Counties. At this point, D2 was removed from Franklin in its entirety, with only a sliver remaining in far NW Moore County, TN. ||By the final issuance for the month of January on the 30th, all categories were reduced by one level, with no areas included in D2 for the first time in 3 months. With this issuance, all counties in Southern Middle TN will be removed from Storm Data as having any Drought Declarations of D2 or higher (triggering their inclusion in Storm Data).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to improve ongoing drought conditions from November 2023 to now with abundant rainfall occurring in December and January. ||While no changes were made from the December 28th issuance for the first Drought Monitor issuance of 2024 on January 4th, rainfall across the area for the first half of January benefitted drought-stricken regions. Minor changes were noted to Extreme Drought (D3) locations in portions of Lawrence and Limestone Counties, but these were minimal. ||Much more improvement was noted by the issuance on the 18th, when Severe Drought (D2) Conditions expanded again from Central portions of the area, now with only slivers of D3 conditions in place across far southern Cullman/Dekalb Counties, and again from SW to NE across a sliver of Colbert and much of NW Lauderdale County. Elsewhere, Severe Drought (D2) Conditions remained except across portions of Morgan, Marshall, Madison, and Jackson Counties in AL, where Moderate Drought (D1) Conditions were present. ||Further improvement was noted by the issuance on the 25th, where conditions were degraded down to Abnormally Dry (D0) across portions of Jackson and Madison Counties. No changes were made elsewhere, with D2 and D3 conditions remaining in aforementioned locations. ||By the final issuance for the month of January on the 30th, all categories were reduced by one level, with no areas included in D3 for the first time in 3 months. With this issuance, all counties except portions of Colbert, Lauderdale, Marshall, and Dekalb Counties will be removed from having Drought Declarations from the Drought Monitor in Storm Data.

The COOP observer in Greeneville reported a light glaze of freezing rain and light snow amidst the rain, and a CoCoRaHS observer near Greeneville reported an event total of roughly one inch of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major snowstorm powered by strong upper level dynamics and widespread preexisting low level cold produced heavy snowfall across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of heavy precipitation fell across the Sevier Smoky Mountains zone during the event, with Gatlinburg and similar lower elevations experiencing rain or freezing rain during the day before a light snow to end the event. Meanwhile the mountains experienced heavy snowfall. The COOP observer on top of Mount LeConte recorded 7 inches of snow, the automated station at Newfound Gap recorded 11.6 inches of snow, and the Sugarlands Visitor Center recorded 2.1 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low brought strong northwest flow winds that induced an orographic snowfall in higher elevations of the East Tennessee mountains and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Newfound Gap automated weather station recorded three inches of snowfall. This leads to the closure of Highway 441 in the National Park. Additionally, light snow was recorded elsewhere in the East Tennessee foothills and mountains, with two inches in Trade, TN, two inches in Roan Mountain, two inches in Rocky Fork, TN, and two inches in Butler, TN.

Public report of 5 inches was measured in Clear Water, a trained spotter in Athens reported 4 inches, a CoCoRaHS observer outside of Athens reported 7 inches of snow, and a CoCoRaHS observer on the north end of the county reported 8 inches of snow and a half inch of sleet and ice. The COOP observer in Athens reported 2.6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major snowstorm powered by strong upper level dynamics and widespread preexisting low level cold produced heavy snowfall across East Tennessee.

The COOP observer in Decatur recorded 7.5 inches of snowfall, and a public report of 6.5 inches was also measured. In Ten Mile, a public report measured 9 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that initiated convection on May 8th continued into the overnight hours of May 9th. Enough lift and instability allowed storms to produce damaging winds and a tornado across West Tennessee. Precipitable water values were above climatology and resulted in locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intermittent tornado initially touched down in a heavily wooded area between Pauline Road and Coxburg Road South approximately two miles north of Interstate 40. The tornado caused intermittent tree damage as it tracked east-southeast over Eagle Creek and briefly lifted west of Coxburg Road South. The tornado touched down again 200 yards east, or just east of Coxburg Road South. The tornado dissipated in a heavily wooded area approximately three-quarters of a mile east-southeast of Coxburg Road South. The total track length was 1.3 miles and 100 yards wide. Peak winds were estimated at 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major snowstorm powered by strong upper level dynamics and widespread preexisting low level cold produced heavy snowfall across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light coating of freezing rain to start the event turned into heavy snowfall for the remaining portion. Morgan County EM reported a light glaze of ice in Wartburg and Lancing at the beginning of the event from freezing rain. A CoCoRaHS observer in Petros reported an event total of 7.3 inches of snowfall. A public person reported an event total of 10 inches of snow in Wartburg. Another public report of 6 inches was received from Lancing.

An off duty NWS Employee reported an event total of 9 inches of snow in Strawberry Plains. A member of the public reported 6 inches in Kodak, and the COOP observer in Sevierville measured 1.5 inches of snow.

Additionally, a trained spotter south of Maynardville recorded 8.3 inches of snow with a fine glaze of ice, and another spotter measured 6.4 inches of snow in Sharps Chapel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major snowstorm powered by strong upper level dynamics and widespread preexisting low level cold produced heavy snowfall across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy, crippling snowfall fell across Knox County, with widespread reports of 8 inches to 12 inches of snow, especially across the west side of the county. A Knoxville based COOP observer measured 11.3 inches of snow, a CoCoRaHS observer in Powell recorded 8.2 inches, another just north of the 640 loop measured 9.4 inches, a CoCoRaHS report west of Knoxville measured 8.7 inches, and a CoCoRaHS observation in Farragut recorded 10.2 inches. Numerous reports on Facebook from all across the county sent measurements, including 9.5 inches at Lakeshore Park, 8.8 inches in Mascot, 9.2 inches in West Knoxville, 10 inches in Bearden, 9 inches in Farragut, amongst dozens of other reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass brought very cold air temperatures and sub-zero wind chills across West Tennessee during the January 14-17, 2024 timeframe. As a surface high shifted directly over the Mid-South, clear skies, light winds, and snowpack left the region bitterly cold with well below zero temperatures across parts of West Tennessee on the morning of January 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values of 0 degrees to 15 below zero. Air temperature fell to 3 degrees below zero on the morning of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough pushed into the Mid-South during the early morning hours. A very moist and unstable air mass provided plenty of fuel for the trough to initiate convection. The atmosphere harnessed steep mid-level lapse rates, plentiful instability, around 50 kts of effective shear. and decent helicity. This type of atmosphere supported all weather hazards. Recent heavy rainfall events also heightened a flood threat. The severe threat continued into the next day as a line of storms rolled through just after midnight into the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began in far southern Madison County along Sanderson Road where several trees were uprooted. The tornado continued eastward along Hailey Road where numerous trees were uprooted or snapped. The first sign of structural damage occurred to the carport of a home on Bowman Road just west of Kelly Road with more widespread and significant tree damage in the field just behind the house. Several trees were uprooted along Swink Road but almost none of the adjacent structures had any damage. Fairly widespread tree damage continued in a rural area on Lowery and Cain Roads as it moved into Chester County. The most significant damage occurred to two houses just off Highway 18, close to the Hardeman County Line. Peak winds were estimated at 95 mph. One house had partial uplift of the roof and notable loss of paneling from the second story. A second house had a tree collapse onto a garage, major uplift of a patio roof, and its chimney collapsed on the side of the house. In many places, the tree damage was sporadic |and consistent with a broad circulation.  The tornado dissipated as it moved across the very northeast corner of Hardeman County and into Chester County. The tornado eventually lifted in a heavily forested area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough pushed into the Mid-South during the early morning hours. A very moist and unstable air mass provided plenty of fuel for the trough to initiate convection. The atmosphere harnessed steep mid-level lapse rates, plentiful instability, around 50 kts of effective shear. and decent helicity. This type of atmosphere supported all weather hazards. Recent heavy rainfall events also heightened a flood threat. The severe threat continued into the next day as a line of storms rolled through just after midnight into the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado that caused damage in southern Madison County crossed into the extreme northeast corner of Hardeman County. The tornado knocked down some trees before traveling into Chester County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major snowstorm powered by strong upper level dynamics and widespread preexisting low level cold produced heavy snowfall across East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation fell mainly as rain in Cocke County for a significant portion of the event, but freezing rain and light snow combined to make transportation extremely hazardous, with the county emergency manager reporting extremely icy roads that were near impassable with several wrecks including tractor trailers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass overspread the region after the recent snowfall. North winds combined with the very cold temperatures to produce dangerous cold weather from the 16th into the 17th. During the morning of the 16th, wind chill values dropped into into the 0 to -10 degree range. During the overnight of the 16th into the morning of the 17th, temperatures dropped into the 0 to -12 degree range for morning lows. Although winds were light, wind chill values dropped briefly (less than an hour) into the -10 to -15 degree range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The overnight low temperature dropped to -10 degrees at Winchester and -2 at Sewanee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low tracked across West Tennessee during the evening of February 12, 2024. Heavy rain changed to heavy wet snow under the core of the upper low as it moved across the area. One to three inches of snow caused slippery roads along and just north of I-40.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of wet snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across West Tennessee during the afternoon of February 16, 2024. The front moved into an area with some instability and numerous showers and thunderstorms developed. A few storms contained large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail fell on Highway 22 south of Lexington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving cold front intersected an area of marginal instability resulting in a line of thunderstorms that raced across the Mid-South during the morning hours of February 28, 2024. There were isolated reports of damaging winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and roof damage occurred in Maury City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impactful winter storm occurred after the passage of an Arctic cold front on the 14th and an upper level disturbance and jet streak tracked east-northeast through the mid South and Tennessee Valley the evening of the 14th into the 15th. This resulted in a band of heavy snow with some sleet mixed in at times that accumulated to 4 to 8 inches in southern middle Tennessee. This resulted in travel disruptions for several days given that near record or record cold temperatures below zero would occur. Temperatures would remain below freezing through the 19th before temperatures warmed into the middle to upper 30s, but then dropped below freezing for a couple more days.  Substantial improvement did not occur until the 23rd or 24th when temperatures stayed above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of around 6 inches were reported. The heaviest was 6.4 inches at Lynchburg (COOP).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted all of Middle Tennessee beginning late Sunday, January 14, 2024. With a very cold airmass already in place, snow fell either into the late hours of January 15th or into the early morning hours of January 16th. Many locations across Middle Tennessee reported storm total snow amounts in excess of 6 inches. There were mainly two heavy snow bands that developed. The first from Humphreys County near the Tennessee River stretching northeast into Wilson County. This area received 6 to 8 inches of snow. The official climate site at Nashville International Airport measured 7.6 inches of snow during this event, receiving more snow in this one event than the city typically receives during the entire winter season. The second band of heavy snow was across the south, stretching from Lawrence County northeast into Cumberland County where totals of 6 to 8 inches were also measured. This heavy snow combined with dangerously cold temperatures that settled in after the snow was over led to days of treacherous conditions for all Middle Tennessee counties as the snow was unable to melt. Numerous traffic accidents were reported all across the area. Unfortunately, this event led to three indirect fatalities, one in Hickman County and two in Marshall County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across Hickman County with various sources reporting 5 to 8 inches of snow. The highest snow report received was from the Centerville area where 8 inches was reported. Unfortunately, this event resulted in one indirect fatality in Hickman County when a 66-year-old fell through a skylight as he was trying to brush snow off of a roof. The heavy snow and winter weather conditions caused water utility issues across Hickman County with the Centertown Water System issuing a Water Boil Advisory.

Unfortunately, a 19 year old male was severely injured with a spine injury while sledding in Fayetteville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneficial moisture across Middle Tennessee led to significant drought improvement in January 2024. By the end of January, zero percent of the area was in extreme drought while 22 percent of the area was in severe drought. This was a marked improvement from December 2023 when over half the area was in at least severe drought. By the end of January, Nashville had officially measured 5.40 inches of precipitation, about 1.38 inches above normal for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from December 2023 improved to severe drought conditions in January 2024.

This was a marked improvement from December 2023 when over half the area was in at least severe drought. By the end of January, Nashville had officially measured 5.40 inches of precipitation, about 1.38 inches above normal for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from December 2023 improved to abnormally dry conditions in January 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong low pressure system tracked through the Great Lakes region of the country, a very strong pressure gradient and a potent low-level jet resulted in strong winds across Middle Tennessee on January 12, 2024. These winds generally ranged from 40 to 55 mph with isolated gusts over 60 mph mainly in Putnam County on the Cumberland Plateau. Numerous reports of downed trees and minor structural damage were received, particularly across counties along the Cumberland Plateau where winds were strongest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds with gusts up to 50 mph downed a few trees across Van Buren County, including on Captain Carter Road and Cane Creek Cumminsville Road in Spencer and Lakeside Drive near Fall Creek Falls. Power outages resulted from these downed trees. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 66 mph was reported in Monterey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong low pressure system tracked through the Great Lakes region of the country, a very strong pressure gradient and a potent low-level jet resulted in strong winds across Middle Tennessee on January 12, 2024. Numerous reports of downed trees and minor structural damage were received, particularly across counties along the Cumberland Plateau where winds were strongest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds across Putnam County resulted in numerous reports of downed trees. Near Cookeville, a tree fell on a house on Farmington Drive while another tree fell onto a home along Cumber Lane. A tree was also reported down on East 13th Street near the Tennessee Tech campus. Near Algood, a tree fell onto a car on Lane Avenue at Warren Avenue, and two trees fell onto a home and chicken coop in the Bangham community. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted all of Middle Tennessee beginning late Sunday, January 14, 2024. With a very cold airmass already in place, snow fell either into the late hours of January 15th or into the early morning hours of January 16th. Many locations across Middle Tennessee reported storm total snow amounts in excess of 6 inches. There were mainly two heavy snow bands that developed. The first from Humphreys County near the Tennessee River stretching northeast into Wilson County. This area received 6 to 8 inches of snow. The official climate site at Nashville International Airport measured 7.6 inches of snow during this event, receiving more snow in this one event than the city typically receives during the entire winter season. The second band of heavy snow was across the south, stretching from Lawrence County northeast into Cumberland County where totals of 6 to 8 inches were also measured. This heavy snow combined with dangerously cold temperatures that settled in after the snow was over led to days of treacherous conditions for all Middle Tennessee counties as the snow was unable to melt. Numerous traffic accidents were reported all across the area. Unfortunately, this event led to three indirect fatalities, one in Hickman County and two in Marshall County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer near Rickman reported 6 inches of snow.

The official climate site at Nashville International Airport measured 7.6 inches of snow during this event, receiving more snow in this one event than the city typically receives during the entire winter season. The second band of heavy snow was across the south, stretching from Lawrence County northeast into Cumberland County where totals of 6 to 8 inches were also measured. This heavy snow combined with dangerously cold temperatures that settled in after the snow was over led to days of treacherous conditions for all Middle Tennessee counties as the snow was unable to melt. Numerous traffic accidents were reported all across the area. Unfortunately, this event led to three indirect fatalities, one in Hickman County and two in Marshall County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across Clay County with various sources reporting between 4 and 7 inches of snow. The highest snow report received was from Celina where 7 inches of snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong low pressure system tracked through the Great Lakes region of the country, a very strong pressure gradient and a potent low-level jet resulted in strong winds across Middle Tennessee on January 12, 2024. These winds generally ranged from 40 to 55 mph with isolated gusts over 60 mph mainly in Putnam County on the Cumberland Plateau. Numerous reports of downed trees and minor structural damage were received, particularly across counties along the Cumberland Plateau where winds were strongest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in downtown Murfreesboro near the square downed a tree onto a garage. In north Murfreesboro, a 40 foot cypress tree was uprooted. Damages are a rough estimate.

This heavy snow forced BNA to order a ground stop for all flights for portions of the afternoon on January 15th, and over one hundred flights were delayed or canceled as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures in Marshall County led to the death of a 25-year-old man inside his mobile home off Nashville Highway. Responders discovered the deceased man on Wednesday, January 17th inside his mobile home, and he is suspected to have died from hypothermia. Ice was found inside his home as was an overturned space heater that was turned off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough pushed into the Mid-South during the early morning hours. A very moist and unstable air mass provided plenty of fuel for the trough to initiate convection. The atmosphere harnessed steep mid-level lapse rates, plentiful instability, around 50 kts of effective shear. and decent helicity. This type of atmosphere supported all weather hazards. Recent heavy rainfall events also heightened a flood threat. The severe threat continued into the next day as a line of storms rolled through just after midnight into the next day.

A strong upper level weather system generated strong cross mountain flow, inducing mountain waves on the leeside of the mountains. These winds brought strong measured gusts to the mountains and foothills of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOAA ATDD weather station at Camp Creek Elementary recorded several hours of severe non-thunderstorm mountain wave wind gusts, with the peak wind of 93 mph being measured around 12 AM local standard time. Additionally, a personal weather station near Shady Valley, Tennessee in Carter County recorded a peak wind gust of 51 mph around 12:44 AM local standard time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level weather system generated strong cross mountain flow, inducing mountain waves on the leeside of the mountains. These winds brought strong measured gusts to the mountains and foothills of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station near Max Patch Mountain recorded a 59 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level weather system generated strong cross mountain flow, inducing mountain waves on the leeside of the mountains. These winds brought strong measured gusts to the mountains and foothills of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cove Mountain wind tower recorded several severe wind gusts over a multiple hour period. The peak wind gust of 85 mph was recorded at 1 AM standard time, though there were several other observations near 80 mph, along with a lull in severe winds between 5 and 9 AM local standard time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level weather system generated strong cross mountain flow, inducing mountain waves on the leeside of the mountains. These winds brought strong measured gusts to the mountains and foothills of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Great Smoky Mountains National Park weather station at Cades Cove recorded a 67 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust. Nearby another weather station within the park at Indian Grave Gap recorded a 52 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust. Additionally at 1 AM local standard time, the Great Smoky Mountains National Park weather station at Look Rock recorded a 51 mph wind gust. An hour and a half prior a 46 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust is recorded at Knoxville TYS ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system to the northwest induced a low level jet and brought strong cross mountain winds over the Appalachian Mountains. This caused a mountain wave event, bringing powerful non-thunderstorm winds to the foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cove Mountain wind tower measured severe non-thunderstorm wind gusts for several hours, peaking at 77 mph at 2 AM local time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level weather system generated strong cross mountain flow, inducing mountain waves on the leeside of the mountains. These winds brought strong measured gusts to the mountains and foothills of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station outside of Citico recorded a 62 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system to the northwest induced a low level jet and brought strong cross mountain winds over the Appalachian Mountains. This caused a mountain wave event, bringing powerful non-thunderstorm winds to the foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOAA ATDD Camp Creek weather station recorded several hours of non-thunderstorm winds gusting into the 60+ mph range, with the peak gust of 69 mph occurring at 3:45 AM local standard time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms dropped southeast through southern middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of the 15th. A brief EF-0 tornado occurred just southwest of Belvidere in Franklin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drone imagery indicated a narrow and brief tornado touched down on a farm adjacent to U.S. Highway 64 southwest of Belvidere. The tornado snapped and uprooted a few trees on the farm before traveling east to a neighboring building materials company. Here, a number of small unsecured manufactured portable barns were shifted about, including one that was toppled over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low-level jet strengthened across Middle Tennessee, surface winds strengthened in response starting during the afternoon of March 25, 2024 and into the early morning hours of March 26th. While measured winds were less than 50 mph, this did result in minor damage including downed power lines in White County and a downed tree in Maury County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in the Sparta area downed power lines along Gaines Road, causing power outages in the area. Strongest winds measured in Sparta were about 40 mph. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low-level jet strengthened across Middle Tennessee, surface winds strengthened in response starting during the afternoon of March 25, 2024 and into the early morning hours of March 26th. While measured winds were less than 50 mph, this did result in minor damage including downed power lines in White County and a downed tree in Maury County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Facebook video showed a tree falling onto a car along Stiverville Road just west of Toll Duggar Road near Culleoka. No injuries were reported. Strongest winds measured across the area were about 30 to 40 mph. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee during the late evening hours of March 14, 2024 and into the early morning hours of March 15th. The first round of thunderstorms were discrete when they moved into southwest Middle Tennessee. It was this first round of thunderstorms that were the strongest of this event with large hail and severe winds reported in Lawrence and Giles counties. The second round was a line of thunderstorms that weakened significantly as they crossed the Tennessee River, but not before causing wind damage in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter size hail southwest of Deerfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued active pattern across Middle Tennessee in March 2024 led to more significant drought improvement. By March 19th, zero percent of the area was in severe or worse drought for the first time since October 2023. By the end of March, only abnormally dry conditions were present across the area, making this the first time since September 2023 with no drought conditions present.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions across Wayne County finally improved to moderate drought conditions on March 19, 2024. By the end of the month, only abnormally dry conditions were present across Wayne County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system with abundant Gulf moisture produced heavy rain, mainly along the spine of the Appalachians thanks to upslope flow in western North Carolina. The heaviest rain impacted streams uphill from Gatlinburg, producing flooding in Gatlinburg, Pigeon Forge, and points downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources also including law enforcement and an NWS employee reported stream and river flooding. Multiple roads closed around Gatlinburg. Water entered a few business locations along West Prong Little Pigeon River in downtown Gatlinburg. Aquarium Bridge closed. Water flooded parking lots of some venues in Pigeon Forge along the same river as the crest moved downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light wintry mix including light freezing rain and snow, combined with the re-freeze of snowmelt, led to significant travel hazards for motorists on wet roads and interstates the night of Jan 18th. A light snow then followed during the day and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain, freezing rain, and snow fell with a half inch of snow measured in the Hardin Valley area of Knoxville. Extreme cold followed, with refreezing of roads each night of snowmelt causing additional road hazards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass overspread the region after the recent snowfall. North winds combined with the very cold temperatures to produce dangerous cold weather from the 16th into the 17th. During the morning of the 16th, wind chill values dropped into into the 0 to -10 degree range. During the overnight of the 16th into the morning of the 17th, temperatures dropped into the 0 to -12 degree range for morning lows. Although winds were light, wind chill values dropped briefly (less than an hour) into the -10 to -15 degree range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The overnight low temperature dropped to -7 degrees at Fayetteville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief moderation in temperatures on the 18th, more Arctic air arrived dropping wind chills once again below zero during the overnight of the 19th into the morning of the 20th, and again on the morning of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped to as low as 12 below during this short period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on May 26, 2024, and into the 27th. The first round of thunderstorms that moved through the area during the morning of the 26th was the strongest, producing damaging winds and very large hail. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped across Middle Tennessee with the most intense damage occurring in Sumner County and along the northern Cumberland Plateau. Hickman and Williamson counties received very large hail of two to three inches in diameter which caused damage to many vehicles. While the second round of thunderstorms late in the evening on the 26th and in the early morning hours of the 27th were not as intense, they did produce sporadic wind damage in the form of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed numerous trees along Heard Ridge Road near Fairview. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought Conditions improved to Severe (D2) Drought Conditions along and south of Interstate 40 across West Tennessee. Severe (D2) Drought and Moderate (D1) Drought conditions continued to improve as precipitation across Tennessee was near or above normal and temperatures were below normal. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on May 26, 2024, and into the 27th. The first round of thunderstorms that moved through the area during the morning of the 26th was the strongest, producing damaging winds and very large hail. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped across Middle Tennessee with the most intense damage occurring in Sumner County and along the northern Cumberland Plateau. Hickman and Williamson counties received very large hail of two to three inches in diameter which caused damage to many vehicles. While the second round of thunderstorms late in the evening on the 26th and in the early morning hours of the 27th were not as intense, they did produce sporadic wind damage in the form of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey found a large 2.5 mile wide by 7 mile long downburst with embedded stronger microbursts that blew down hundreds of trees across northeast Fentress and eastern Pickett Counties, especially in Pickett State Park and Big South Fork Recreation Area. The park was temporarily closed due to all roads being blocked by downed trees. Max winds were estimated up to 85 mph. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light wintry mix including light freezing rain and snow, combined with the re-freeze of snowmelt, led to significant travel hazards for motorists on wet roads and interstates the night of Jan 18th. A light snow then followed during the day and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across West Tennessee during the afternoon of February 16, 2024. The front moved into an area with some instability and numerous showers and thunderstorms developed. A few storms contained large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny-sized hail fell at Highway 218 and Highway 79.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving low pressure over the Upper Mississippi Valley combined with high pressure over the southeast Atlantic coast created a strong pressure gradient across the Mid-South. This resulted in an extended period of wind gusts over 40 mph across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused structural damage to a historical building at 100 Spring Street in Covington. A partial building collapse occurred. The building was vacant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a major winter storm impacted Middle Tennessee earlier in the week, a trailing weak system then brought another round of wintry weather to the area on January 18 and 19, 2024. Several days of sub-freezing temperatures finally warmed to just above freezing at the surface on January 18th while very cold temperatures remained aloft. This created a favorable profile for freezing rain or freezing drizzle. Despite this precipitation being light, it falling on existing snow cover made already icy conditions worse. Unfortunately, these icy and snowy conditions led to a vehicle crash and a fatality in Coffee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell on top of already existing heavy snow which exacerbated slick and icy conditions. There was one indirect fatality in Coffee County when a 73-year-old man traveling failed to stay in his lane on Hoover Road. The vehicle traveled off the left-hand side of the roadway and collided with two wooden fence posts, which caused the vehicle to catch on fire and become engulfed in flames.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneficial moisture across Middle Tennessee continued to lead to significant drought improvement in February 2024. By the end of February, only about 10 percent of the area was in severe drought which was an improvement from the 34 percent in January. By the end of February, Nashville's official climate site had measured 9.21 inches of rain so far this year which is above normal for the end of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from January 2024 improved in February with just the northwest portion of Giles County still in severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were felt across Middle Tennessee on February 27, 2024, with gusts of 35 to 50 mph. These winds were responsible for a downed tree near Springfield in Robertson County. Unfortunately, this tree fell onto a roadway, causing a collision which killed the driver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with saturated grounds from earlier rainfall led to a tree falling in the 3900 block of Kinneys Road just outside of Springfield. The tree fell right in front of a car driven by a 54-year-old woman who collided with the tree. Unfortunately, the collision killed the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a squall of strong to severe thunderstorms across East Tennessee, with ambient non-thunderstorm winds near or at high wind criteria. After the low passed, wrap around snow producing light accumulations occurred across higher elevations of the East TN Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down by severe thunderstorm wind gusts between Tusculum and Mosheim. Additionally, multiple strong wind gusts were recorded during the storms' passage, including 46 mph in Madisonville, 55 mph at Knoxville McGhee Tyson Airport, 56 mph at Sevierville - Pigeon Forge Airport, 40 mph at Greenville AWOS, and 52 mph in Leesburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private weather station near Max Patch Mountain recorded a 62 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cove Mountain wind tower recorded several high wind gusts, beginning with a 62 mph wind gust then ending the following morning with a peak gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved into Middle Tennessee on February 12, 2024. This was the fourth consecutive day of rain across the area, so grounds were already saturated. An estimated one to two inches of rain fell very quickly in the Cookeville and Algood areas in Putnam County which caused flooding in poor drainage and low-lying areas. Several roads in the area were rendered impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Cookeville and Algood areas caused flooding along several streets. As reported by the emergency managers, Free Hill Road, Choate Cemetary Road, Jefferson Avenue, Dirty Road, Dogwood Drive in Cookeville were impassable. In Algood, Main Street and Patton Street were also impassable. Flooding was also reported in several spots along Highway 135 in Putnam County. No damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted the Mid-South on January 14-15, 2024. Transportation was greatly affected. Snow spread across the Mid-South during the afternoon hours on January 14th as an upper jet intensified across the region in advance of a potent upper trough. By early morning Monday, areas along and south of I-40 and especially near the Tennessee/Mississippi border were located under the right entrance region of the upper jet streak. The lift associated with the jet streak combined with a steady influx of mid-level moisture and strong mid-level frontogenesis resulted in heavy snowfall. The heaviest snowfall in West Tennessee fell from I-40 south to the Tennessee/Mississippi state line where four to eight inches fell. The entrance region of the upper jet slowly sagged south through north Mississippi during the day on January 15th and snow gradually tapered off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around seven inches of snow fell across Hardeman County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneficial moisture across Middle Tennessee continued to lead to significant drought improvement in February 2024. By the end of February, only about 10 percent of the area was in severe drought which was an improvement from the 34 percent in January. By the end of February, Nashville's official climate site had measured 9.21 inches of rain so far this year which is above normal for the end of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from January 2024 continued in February although southern portions of Wayne County improved to moderate drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cutoff low over the North Plains, coupled with intense winds aloft and a cold front at the surface, helped spawn multiple rounds of severe thunderstorms that produced hail, flash flooding, and damaging winds to East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Potent thunderstorm downburst winds caused severe damage. Significant roof damage occurred to the Russellville Elementary and subsequent water intrusion into several classrooms and gymnasium. Other structural roof damage occurred to some homes and several tree trunks were snapped with just a few trees uprooted. The Morristown Memorial Gardens Mausoleum also sustained roof damage. The Bethesda Cemetery sustained numerous trees snapped. The total area affected was roughly two square miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure from a mid-Janaury cold snap remained anchored in place across the Mid-South. A weak mid-level shortwave moved into the Mid-South on January 18, 2024, as a weak surface low-pressure system tracked from the Red River Valley across southern Arkansas. As a result, overrunning developed across the area during the early morning hours. Temperatures were below freezing and light freezing rain/freezing drizzle fell across the area resulting in slick roads and numerous accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of ice accumulated on roadways and elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying MCS/MCV moved through the Mid-South during the overnight/early morning hours of May 22. A slow moving cold front was stretched over the Missouri Bootheel and moisture surged northward allowing for plentiful instability. This instability collided with remnant outflow boundaries from previous convection. Peak diurnal heating hours, moisture, and enough lift caused damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 13 roadways in and around the city of Camden that are inundated with water closed to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough pushed into the Mid-South during the early morning hours. A very moist and unstable air mass provided plenty of fuel for the trough to initiate convection. The atmosphere harnessed steep mid-level lapse rates, plentiful instability, around 50 kts of effective shear. and decent helicity. This type of atmosphere supported all weather hazards. Recent heavy rainfall events also heightened a flood threat. The severe threat continued into the next day as a line of storms rolled through just after midnight into the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down off of Jones Bend Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions and Extreme (D3) drought conditions continued along and south of Interstate 40 across West Tennessee, during much of January as below-normal rainfall and warmer-than-normal temperatures started the month. Mid to late month a cold snap and heavy snow event brought wetter conditions and below-normal temperatures to Tennessee. Lake and river levels started the month below normal flow, but upstream snowmelt was able to increase flow to near and above normal.  Drought conditions improved toward the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Drought (D3) conditions improved by the end of the month.

Snow spread across the Mid-South during the afternoon hours on January 14th as an upper jet intensified across the region in advance of a potent upper trough. By early morning Monday, areas along and south of I-40 and especially near the Tennessee/Mississippi border were located under the right entrance region of the upper jet streak. The lift associated with the jet streak combined with a steady influx of mid-level moisture and strong mid-level frontogenesis resulted in heavy snowfall. The heaviest snowfall in West Tennessee fell from I-40 south to the Tennessee/Mississippi state line where four to eight inches fell. The entrance region of the upper jet slowly sagged south through north Mississippi during the day on January 15th and snow gradually tapered off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to seven inches fell across Shelby County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that initiated convection on May 8th continued into the overnight hours of May 9th. Enough lift and instability allowed storms to produce damaging winds and a tornado across West Tennessee. Precipitable water values were above climatology and resulted in locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Ward Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying MCS/MCV moved through the Mid-South during the overnight/early morning hours of May 22. A slow moving cold front was stretched over the Missouri Bootheel and moisture surged northward allowing for plentiful instability. This instability collided with remnant outflow boundaries from previous convection. Peak diurnal heating hours, moisture, and enough lift caused damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several motorists reporting quarter sized hail along HWY51 between Troy and Union City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough pushed into the Mid-South during the early morning hours. A very moist and unstable air mass provided plenty of fuel for the trough to initiate convection. The atmosphere harnessed steep mid-level lapse rates, plentiful instability, around 50 kts of effective shear. and decent helicity. This type of atmosphere supported all weather hazards. Recent heavy rainfall events also heightened a flood threat. The severe threat continued into the next day as a line of storms rolled through just after midnight into the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down blocking the intersection of Lillard and Bobbitt Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions and Extreme (D3) drought conditions continued along and south of Interstate 40 across West Tennessee, during much of January as below-normal rainfall and warmer-than-normal temperatures started the month. Mid to late month a cold snap and heavy snow event brought wetter conditions and below-normal temperatures to Tennessee. Lake and river levels started the month below normal flow, but upstream snowmelt was able to increase flow to near and above normal.  Drought conditions improved toward the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) conditions improved by the middle of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low pressure system moved from the Southern Plains into the Mid-Mississippi Valley while a cold front slowly pressed southeast into the Mid-South during the afternoon of March 14th into the early morning hours of March 15th. The airmass ahead of this system was quite unstable with Mixed Layer Convective Available Potential Energy values of over 2000 J/kg with enough wind shear to support organized convection. Initially, supercells developed over northeast Mississippi and parts of West Tennessee. These storms dropped some large hail and caused some wind damage. Later on in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system pushed across the entire Mid-South resulting in more hail but mostly wind damage. A weak tornado occurred in New Albany, Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were knocked down on Woodrow and Rich Roads.

As a surface high shifted directly over the Mid-South, clear skies, light winds, and snowpack left the region bitterly cold with well below zero temperatures across parts of West Tennessee on the morning of January 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values of 0 degrees to 15 below zero. Air temperature fell to 8 degrees below zero on the morning of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure from a mid-Janaury cold snap remained anchored in place across the Mid-South. A weak mid-level shortwave moved into the Mid-South on January 18, 2024, as a weak surface low-pressure system tracked from the Red River Valley across southern Arkansas. As a result, overrunning developed across the area during the early morning hours. Temperatures were below freezing and light freezing rain/freezing drizzle fell across the area resulting in slick roads and numerous accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of ice accumulated on roadways and elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted the Mid-South on January 14-15, 2024. Transportation was greatly affected. Snow spread across the Mid-South during the afternoon hours on January 14th as an upper jet intensified across the region in advance of a potent upper trough. By early morning Monday, areas along and south of I-40 and especially near the Tennessee/Mississippi border were located under the right entrance region of the upper jet streak. The lift associated with the jet streak combined with a steady influx of mid-level moisture and strong mid-level frontogenesis resulted in heavy snowfall. The heaviest snowfall in West Tennessee fell from I-40 south to the Tennessee/Mississippi state line where four to eight inches fell. The entrance region of the upper jet slowly sagged south through north Mississippi during the day on January 15th and snow gradually tapered off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches fell across Tipton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying MCS/MCV moved through the Mid-South during the overnight/early morning hours of May 22. A slow moving cold front was stretched over the Missouri Bootheel and moisture surged northward allowing for plentiful instability. This instability collided with remnant outflow boundaries from previous convection. Peak diurnal heating hours, moisture, and enough lift caused damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down across the northeast portion of Lauderdale county. Trees down at intersection of Edit Nankipoo Road and Cook Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough pushed into the Mid-South during the early morning hours. A very moist and unstable air mass provided plenty of fuel for the trough to initiate convection. The atmosphere harnessed steep mid-level lapse rates, plentiful instability, around 50 kts of effective shear. and decent helicity. This type of atmosphere supported all weather hazards. Recent heavy rainfall events also heightened a flood threat. The severe threat continued into the next day as a line of storms rolled through just after midnight into the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 25 utility poles downed in Tiptonville and surrounding. Widespread tree damage and some structural damages including a metal building lost its roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions and Extreme (D3) drought conditions continued along and south of Interstate 40 across West Tennessee, during much of January as below-normal rainfall and warmer-than-normal temperatures started the month. Mid to late month a cold snap and heavy snow event brought wetter conditions and below-normal temperatures to Tennessee. Lake and river levels started the month below normal flow, but upstream snowmelt was able to increase flow to near and above normal.  Drought conditions improved toward the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Drought (D3) conditions improved by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South was pinched in the warm sector of two frontal boundaries in the early afternoon hours allowing a few strong to severe thunderstorms across Tennessee. A strong cold front aimed to cross the Mid-South later that evening with a primed environment that featured steep lapse rates, plentiful instability, curved hodographs, and plenty of effective shear. Strong upper level divergence and an intensifying low level jet combined with the environment to produce damaging winds and large hail. Convection continued until shortly after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reports 2.00 inch hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was felled by severe thunderstorm winds. Additionally, roof shingles from the church next door were stripped off the roof. A personal weather station recorded a 60 mph wind gust during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active storm pattern as several mid-level shortwaves crossed the region. An MCV lifted east northeast out of the Ozarks across southern Arkansas. The MCV fired up morning convection across the Mid-South with isolated storm coverage in the afternoon. A weak capped environment was broken and steep mid-level lapse rates, roughly 30 knots of shear, and plentiful instability allowed storms to flourish to the south. High Precipitable Water values and training storms resulted in several instances of flash flooding across West Tennessee resulting in evacuations, a stalled vehicle, and leaving several roads closed or impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 89 closed between State Route 105 and the Obion County line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pond Creek overflowed and spread water across Johnny Powell Rd and Highway 189, north of Maury City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying MCS/MCV moved through the Mid-South during the overnight/early morning hours of May 22. A slow moving cold front was stretched over the Missouri Bootheel and moisture surged northward allowing for plentiful instability. This instability collided with remnant outflow boundaries from previous convection. Peak diurnal heating hours, moisture, and enough lift caused damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped about two feet off the ground in Medina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low from central Missouri aided by a surface low over the Missouri Bootheel pinched off a warm sector ahead of a cold front. This warm sector provided enough instability and weak shear to produce a few strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. Sub-severe hail and winds accompanied a few severe hail storms along the Tennessee and  Mississippi border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail reported in Crump.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South was pinched in the warm sector of two frontal boundaries in the early afternoon hours allowing a few strong to severe thunderstorms across Tennessee. A strong cold front aimed to cross the Mid-South later that evening with a primed environment that featured steep lapse rates, plentiful instability, curved hodographs, and plenty of effective shear. Strong upper level divergence and an intensifying low level jet combined with the environment to produce damaging winds and large hail. Convection continued until shortly after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reports 1.00 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions and Extreme (D3) drought conditions continued along and south of Interstate 40 across West Tennessee, during much of January as below-normal rainfall and warmer-than-normal temperatures started the month. Mid to late month a cold snap and heavy snow event brought wetter conditions and below-normal temperatures to Tennessee. Lake and river levels started the month below normal flow, but upstream snowmelt was able to increase flow to near and above normal.  Drought conditions improved toward the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Drought (D3) conditions worsened to Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions early in the month and then improved back to Extreme Drought (D3) conditions by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low pressure system moved from the Southern Plains into the Mid-Mississippi Valley while a cold front slowly pressed southeast into the Mid-South during the afternoon of March 14th into the early morning hours of March 15th. The airmass ahead of this system was quite unstable with Mixed Layer Convective Available Potential Energy values of over 2000 J/kg with enough wind shear to support organized convection. Initially, supercells developed over northeast Mississippi and parts of West Tennessee. These storms dropped some large hail and caused some wind damage. Later on in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system pushed across the entire Mid-South resulting in more hail but mostly wind damage. A weak tornado occurred in New Albany, Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were knocked down on Dew Drop Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to Moderate (D1) drought conditions across Hardin County Tennessee by the middle of March. Several locally heavy rainfall events were able to enhance streamflow and improve conditions to Abnormally Dry (D0) by the end of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to Moderate (D1) drought conditions by the middle of the month. Improvement continued to Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions by the end of March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that initiated convection on May 8th continued into the overnight hours of May 9th. Enough lift and instability allowed storms to produce damaging winds and a tornado across West Tennessee. Precipitable water values were above climatology and resulted in locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two barns lost their roofs. One barn was on Poplar Springs Bargerton Road and the other barn was on Poplar Springs Juno Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered, slow-moving convection beneath a humid high pressure ridge produced isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding in and around the Dollywood theme park turned several of the park's walkways into streams, flooding several shops within the park. Excessive water ponded in the parking lot, flooding numerous vehicles. Nearby, Veterans Blvd was closed from McCarter Hollow Road to Forge Hideaway Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered, slow-moving convection beneath a humid high pressure ridge produced isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Barnard Narrows Road at Decatur Highway was closed by flooding to a two-foot depth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped just northwest of the area, while southwesterly flow aloft was adding moisture to the vertical column. Early evening convection produced localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was covering roads near the intersection of Martin Mill Pike and Tipton Station Road, and the intersection of Brown Road and Rudder Road. One car was stranded in water in a nearby subdivision. Tipton Station Road was also flooded near South Doyle High School.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads flooded at the intersection of Neubert Springs Road and West Ford Valley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached 105 degrees in Lincoln County during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index reached 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid air mass produced diurnal convection that resulted in localized flash flooding at the University of Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake Avenue and 19th Street flooded to a depth of three inches near UTK campus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally dry month of June 2024 led to flash drought conditions in the month of July 2024. By July 9th, severe drought conditions developed in Lawrence, Giles, Marshall, Maury, and Cumberland counties, or about 13 percent of Middle Tennessee. Drought conditions expanded a week later on July 16th with about 32 percent of the area in at least severe drought. Southern portions of Lawrence and Giles deteriorated to extreme drought conditions at the point as well. Beneficial rainfall during the latter half of July, however, helped to improve drought conditions so that by the end of the month, only southern Lawrence and Giles counties remained in severe drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across Cumberland County during the month of July 2024, peaking to severe drought conditions in mid-July. By the end of the month, drought conditions improved to moderate drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge ridge covered the southern U.S. during early July. Meanwhile, moisture surged northward into the Mid-South ahead of an approaching cold front. Dewpoints climbed well into the 70s and afternoon heat index values topped 110 degrees across the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values topped 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on July 17, 2024. The main hazard with these thunderstorms were strong winds with several trees blown down across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and a 40-foot TV tower were blown down at Dale Hollow Lake. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Summertime thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on July 28, 2024. One of these typical summertime storms produced heavy rain in Macon County, causing flooding in the Lafayette area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Lafayette area caused flash flooding, forcing the closure of several roads in the area. Damages are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Summertime thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on July 28, 2024. One of these typical summertime storms produced heavy rain in Macon County, causing flooding in the Lafayette area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding reported over several roads in southern Van Buren County. Roads included Old State Highway 111, Howard, Brockdell, and Fall Creek Road. Several pictures received from local emergency manager. Received a couple reports of rainfall over 5 inches in the vicinity, all of which occurred within a 2 hour time frame. Damages are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on July 29, 2024. The most impactful thunderstorm of the day was located in the Murfreesboro area. A microburst with estimated 80 mph winds caused tree and structural damage in the area. Meanwhile, an EF-0 tornado touched down on Middle Tennessee State University's campus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small, weak EF-0 tornado moved through Murfreesboro, also impacting Middle Tennessee State University. Main damage was large tree branches down with some trees uprooted. Minor structural damage was noted at the stadium, including some siding material off of one of the buildings and some gate and fencing damage. The tornado began just east of Memorial Boulevard, continued east-southeast towards Middle Tennessee State University, and ended southeast of campus on Copper Hollow Drive. Maximum winds were estimated to be 75 mph. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of intense damaging winds up to 80 mph occurred across central Murfreesboro from around the Stones River to downtown. There was some minor roof damage to a few buildings, including metal peeled up at a business and some shingles lost on a few houses. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of thunderstorms moved through Middle Tennessee on July 30, 2024. One of these lines moved through the area during the afternoon while the second arrived late in the evening. Damaging winds were the primary threat with these thunderstorms with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines received. Heavy rain was also reported across the Cumberland Plateau with flooding occurring in portions of Putnam and White counties. In Williamson County, a lightning strike caused a house fire which caused smoke inhalation for four firefighters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed a tree along W Bockman Way in Sparta. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of thunderstorms moved through Middle Tennessee on July 30, 2024. One of these lines moved through the area during the afternoon while the second arrived late in the evening. Damaging winds were the primary threat with these thunderstorms with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines received. Heavy rain was also reported across the Cumberland Plateau with flooding occurring in portions of Putnam and White counties. In Williamson County, a lightning strike caused a house fire which caused smoke inhalation for four firefighters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed a tree along Highway 70 in Rome. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge and approaching shortwave trough surged moisture across the area to allow dewpoints to creep into the low to mid 70s with hot conditions in place. Fortunately, convection and excellent mixing allowed heat indices to remain below excessive levels. Nonetheless, hot and humid conditions persisted ahead of a cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices between 105 and 109 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the remnants of Hurricane Beryl moved northward from the Gulf Coast moisture streamed into the Mid-South. This moisture combined with strong surface heating to produce an isolated severe storm with large hail during the early afternoon of July 8, 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level high-pressure system covered the Lower Mississippi Valley at the end of July. Persistent moist southwest flow brought high dewpoints into the region. Hot temperatures combined with dewpoints well into the 70s resulted in widespread afternoon heat index values above 110 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values topped 110 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values topped 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of thunderstorms moved through Middle Tennessee on July 30, 2024. One of these lines moved through the area during the afternoon while the second arrived late in the evening. Damaging winds were the primary threat with these thunderstorms with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines received. Heavy rain was also reported across the Cumberland Plateau with flooding occurring in portions of Putnam and White counties. In Williamson County, a lightning strike caused a house fire which caused smoke inhalation for four firefighters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An apparent microburst caused a brick storage building to collapse at Highway 41 and East College Street in Greenbrier. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough extended from the Great Lakes to northeast Texas. An upper-level disturbance moving through the Mid-South ignited convection across the Memphis metropolitan area during the late morning hours on July 25, 2024. A very moist airmass was in place across the region with anomalous precipitable water values of over 2 inches. The thunderstorms were slow moving resulting in heavy rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of southeast Memphis. There were several reports of water up to the doors of vehicles including near Ridgeway Boulevard and Hickory Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge and approaching shortwave trough surged moisture across the area to allow dewpoints to creep into the low to mid 70s with hot conditions in place. Fortunately, convection and excellent mixing allowed heat indices to remain below excessive levels. Nonetheless, hot and humid conditions persisted ahead of a cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices between 105 and 109 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally dry month of June 2024 led to flash drought conditions in the month of July 2024. By July 9th, severe drought conditions developed in Lawrence, Giles, Marshall, Maury, and Cumberland counties, or about 13 percent of Middle Tennessee. Drought conditions expanded a week later on July 16th with about 32 percent of the area in at least severe drought. Southern portions of Lawrence and Giles deteriorated to extreme drought conditions at the point as well. Beneficial rainfall during the latter half of July, however, helped to improve drought conditions so that by the end of the month, only southern Lawrence and Giles counties remained in severe drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across Lawrence County during the month of July 2024, peaking to extreme drought conditions in mid-July. By the end of the month, drought conditions improved to severe drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge ridge covered the southern U.S. during early July. Meanwhile, moisture surged northward into the Mid-South ahead of an approaching cold front. Dewpoints climbed well into the 70s and afternoon heat index values topped 110 degrees across the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values topped 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Drought Conditions developed in early July thanks to less than normal rainfall for the area. Should drier conditions persist, drought conditions will worsen this Summer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Drought conditions developed for a sizeable portion of North Central Alabama, including portions of Lauderdale, Limestone, Madison, and Jackson Counties in Northern AL. In Southern Middle TN, Lincoln, Moore, and Franklin Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across the area and D3 Conditions were introduced for portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As of 7/16, D3 Drought Conditions were introduced for portions of Lauderdale, Limestone, and Madison Counties in Northern Alabama. In Southern Middle TN, this includes Lincoln County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Due to recent rainfall, D3 conditions improved and were downgraded to D2 in Lincoln County, TN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D3 Conditions were downgraded to D2 thanks to recent rainfall across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally dry month of June 2024 led to flash drought conditions in the month of July 2024. Drought conditions expanded a week later on July 16th with about 32 percent of the area in at least severe drought. Southern portions of Lawrence and Giles deteriorated to extreme drought conditions at the point as well. Beneficial rainfall during the latter half of July, however, helped to improve drought conditions so that by the end of the month, only southern Lawrence and Giles counties remained in severe drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across Maury County during the month of July 2024, peaking to severe drought conditions in mid-July. By the end of the month, drought conditions improved to moderate drought conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across Marshall County during the month of July 2024, peaking to severe drought conditions in mid-July. By the end of the month, drought conditions improved to moderate drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally dry month of June 2024 led to flash drought conditions in the month of July 2024. By July 9th, severe drought conditions developed in Lawrence, Giles, Marshall, Maury, and Cumberland counties, or about 13 percent of Middle Tennessee. Drought conditions expanded a week later on July 16th with about 32 percent of the area in at least severe drought. Southern portions of Lawrence and Giles deteriorated to extreme drought conditions at the point as well. Beneficial rainfall during the latter half of July, however, helped to improve drought conditions so that by the end of the month, only southern Lawrence and Giles counties remained in severe drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across Bedford County during the month of July 2024, peaking to severe drought conditions in mid-July. By the end of the month, drought conditions improved to moderate drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge and approaching shortwave trough surged moisture across the area to allow dewpoints to creep into the low to mid 70s with hot conditions in place. Fortunately, convection and excellent mixing allowed heat indices to remain below excessive levels. Nonetheless, hot and humid conditions persisted ahead of a cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices between 105 and 109 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on June 3, 2024. The strongest thunderstorm impacted the Nashville area where numerous trees fell, and flash flooding occurred in the Inglewood neighborhood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across portions of Robertson and Sumner counties during the early morning hours of June 30, 2024. Official measurements showed 3 to 3.5 inches of rain in a short period of time, and the White House area was hardest hit by flooding with numerous homes reported flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across portions of Sumner County during the early morning hours of June 30th. CoCoRaHS observers in this area measured 3 to 3.5 inches of rain which caused flooding. The White House area was hardest hit with flooding. In White House, roads that were flooded included Highway 31, Rolling Acres Drive, Union Road, Winchester Court, and Autumn Wood Drive. Along Autumn Wood Drive, one home was flooded, and a car was overturned. Homes were also flooded on Winchester Court and Rolling Acres Drive. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist, tropical-like, airmass covered the Mid-South on June 17, 2024. A weak mid-level shortwave rotating around the western periphery of the upper-level ridge situated west of the Mid-South.  This feature interacted with the very moist and unstable airmass in place resulting in numerous showers and thunderstorms. A slow-moving heavy thunderstorm resulted in flash flooding in East Memphis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Poplar Avenue and International Drive intersection was flooded. Three cars were stranded.

The aforementioned upper ridge continued to intensify resulting in a couple of days of dangerous heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices in between 105 and 109 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low was situated near St. Louis, Missouri with a quasi-stationary front extending southeast into West Tennessee and northern Alabama. Aloft, a broad shortwave trough was present over the Lower Mississippi Valley. Isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the region as surface heating intensified on June 1, 2024. A thunderstorm near Kenton produced a landspout in extreme northern Gibson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout was observed in extreme northern Gibson County about one mile west of Kenton. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south from northern Missouri during the evening hours of June 8, 2024. A mesoscale convective system developed along and in advance of the front in the moist and unstable airmass across southern Missouri. There was enough shear across the area to support a few severe storms. This convection pushed across northern parts of the Mid-South mainly during the early morning hours of June 9, 2024, with isolated reports of hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down in town.

Upper-level ridging prevailed from the southwest U.S. eastward across the southern states. Moisture pooled along a stalled frontal boundary across the Mid-South with dewpoints rising into the upper 70s. The abundance of moisture combined with temperatures around 100 degrees resulted in widespread high heat indices.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices in between 105 and 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low-pressure system moved across the Central Plains while the associated cold front pushed into the Mid-South from the west on April 7, 2024. A narrow ribbon of moisture advected northward ahead of the front resulting in enough instability for severe storms. Meanwhile, an 80-knot mid-level speed max pushing into the region provided the wind shear for storm organization. Several severe storms occurred from extreme northeast Mississippi across West Tennessee producing large hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracked across the Mid-Mississippi Valley on April 2, 2024. A broad warm sector was in place across the Mid-South. A lead shortwave ejecting out of the upper trough over northern Mexico interacted with the moist and unstable airmass in place across the region. A cluster of thunderstorms developed over southern Arkansas during the early morning hours of April 2nd. A 40-knot low-level jet helped to fuel the storms as they tracked northeastward. Storms strengthened across extreme northwest Mississippi and West Tennessee resulting in a few reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped in the Brazil community. There was minor shingle damage in the Brazil community.

An isolated thunderstorm moved across far northwest Middle Tennessee during the late afternoon of April 8, 2024. With abundant instability, this thunderstorm became severe in west Tennessee before crossing the Tennessee River. Reports of large hail up to 1.5 inches were received along with damage due to strong winds across portions of Houston and Stewart counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds blew a roof off of a small barn in Tennessee Ridge. Damages are a rough estimate.

Reports of large hail up to 1.5 inches were received along with damage due to strong winds across portions of Houston and Stewart counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced ping pong ball size hail in Cumberland City.

A strong pressure gradient developed across the region in the wake of a departing surface low. An 8-millibar gradient across the Mid-South resulted in northwest winds with gusts to 45 mph. A few trees were knocked down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell across Interstate 40, blocking traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracked across the Mid-Mississippi Valley on April 2, 2024. A broad warm sector was in place across the Mid-South. A lead shortwave ejecting out of the upper trough over northern Mexico interacted with the moist and unstable airmass in place across the region. A cluster of thunderstorms developed over southern Arkansas during the early morning hours of April 2nd. A 40-knot low-level jet helped to fuel the storms as they tracked northeastward. Storms strengthened across extreme northwest Mississippi and West Tennessee resulting in a few reports of large hail and damaging winds.

A potent storm system tracked across the central United States on April 2, 2024. Despite a primed atmosphere that included CAPE values of 1,000 to 1,500 J/kg, 50 to 70 knots of bulk shear, and a large, curved hodograph that indicated abundant helicity, thunderstorms largely failed to take advantage of all of these parameters across Middle Tennessee. Two limiting factors could have been lapse rates of only 6 to 6.5 Celsius per kilometer and the lack of a mid-level dry layer. So fortunately, thunderstorms across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours were generally sub-severe. The strongest storm of the day was in Robertson County where downed trees and power lines resulted in power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Facebook user reported a tree down at Jernigan Road and Archer Road near Cross Plains. Other trees and power lines were reported downed in the Cross Plains and Springfield area, leaving about 1,300 customers without power. Damages are a rough estimate.
