EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant outbreak of tornadoes occurred in two waves during the evening of the 8th and early morning hours of the 9th. The first wave occurred as multiple supercell thunderstorms developed across southern middle Tennessee and far north central Alabama. These storms moved east during the evening hours, producing ## tornadoes in southern middle Tennessee.||A break in the activity was then followed by a line of thunderstorms that dropped southeast through southern middle Tennessee into north central and northeast Alabama after Midnight through the early morning hours. The line produced ## tornadoes in southern middle Tennessee, a couple of which continued into northeast Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the Blanche Road area between Philpot Road and Cheatham Road. Numerous hardwood trees were uprooted and trunks were snapped with one tree on a house. The tornado continued across Randolph Hill Road then produced more hardwood tree damage across Sanders Road. The tornado followed Dry Creek, south of Poorhouse Road, where numerous hardwood trees were snapped through Clay Jones Road. More significant damage, including minor roof damage to a single-family house and more dense areas of tree snaps, occurred east of Clay Jones Road. This is the location where the maximum winds of up to 105 mph occurred.  The tornado lifted east of this residence in a valley west of Drennon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system, complete with a sweeping cold front, generated thunderstorms across Tennessee and the wider region. One discrete supercell thunderstorm produced a tornado in the northern portions of the Cumberland Plateau in East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began near Summers Hill Road and Deer Lodge Highway producing EF-1 damage in Downtown Sunbright with damage to numerous residential and commercial structures. The tornado continued northeast across White Oak Creek crossing the railroad tracks near the intersection of Dynatex Rd and Raymond Webb Road. There was damage to some metal building structures, hardwood trees, and barns in this area. Primary structure damage along the path of the tornado was uplift, peeling, and partial removal of roofs. The tornado re-intensified and tracked along Mill Creek Road producing damage to several homes and barns before the tornado dissipated near Davis Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked from Lincoln County with damage noted along Elora Road, continuing southwest of Huntland just east of Stovall Mountain. In this location of Greasy Cove, the tornado uprooted and snapped several large hardwood oak trees, and was at its most intense along Whites Gap Road. Here, the tornado produced 105 mph winds, destroying a pole barn in addition to snapping the very large oak trees that were 6-8 feet in diameter. The tornado lifted shortly after crossing Whites Gap Road along Hall Branch, about a quarter of a mile north of the Alabama/Tennessee State  |line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Francine continued to weaken as it neared the Mid-South. Francine weakened to a post-tropical cyclone on its northerly track. A corridor of up to 50 mph wind gusts developed across north Mississippi near the Tennessee and Mississippi Border. One high wind gust was noted in Shelby County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was reported at Memphis International.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Windy conditions developed during the late morning and midday hours as Tropical Depression Francine lifted northward through central and northern Mississippi.  East to southeast winds increased to 25-35 mph with frequent gusts of 40-50 mph.  The winds knocked down numerous trees and tree limbs throughout north Alabama and southern middle Tennessee, impacting several power lines and inducing power outages affecting tens of thousands.  A few trees fell on vehicles.  No injuries have been reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down at no less than six locations in Lincoln County.  The trees blocked roadways at all six locations and pulled power lines down at one location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on September 1, 2024. Lightning with one of these thunderstorms reportedly struck a man near Antioch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning reportedly struck and injured a 25-year-old man in the chest near Antioch. The man refused medical attention and exact injuries are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Francine made landfall in Louisiana on September 11, 2024, and then moved into the southeast United States. While its path remained to the west of Middle Tennessee, heavy rainbands did bring rain to portions of the area. The highest rain amounts were received across the southwest, particularly in Lawrence County which caused minor flooding in the area. A few strong wind gusts were also reported which caused minor tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from the remnants of tropical cyclone Francine downed a large tree limb on Maple Hill Drive, barely missing a vehicle, in Tullahoma. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from the remnants of tropical cyclone Francine downed a large tree and power lines which blocked N Military Avenue at 3rd Street in Lawrenceburg. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Days of steady rain from the remnants of tropical cyclone Francine produced high rainfall totals across southwest Middle Tennessee. The highest rainfall amount came from a CoCoRaHS observer near St. Joseph who measured 8.28 inches of rain from September 12th until September 16th. This heavy rain caused some area roads across Lawrence County to be impassable at times with one secondary road partially washed out by flowing water. Some local creeks also rose out of their banks. Damages are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that moved eastward through Middle Tennessee on September 24, 2024. With plenty of instability and wind shear, these thunderstorms strengthened and became severe through the afternoon and evening. Damaging winds were the primary threat with numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines received. The most significant damage was reported in Ethridge where severe winds caused a tent to collapse, injuring three people. Some hail reports were received as well, but these were mostly less than one inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed trees onto powerlines near White Bluff. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant outbreak of tornadoes occurred in two waves during the evening of the 8th and early morning hours of the 9th. These storms moved east during the evening hours, producing ## tornadoes in southern middle Tennessee.||A break in the activity was then followed by a line of thunderstorms that dropped southeast through southern middle Tennessee into north central and northeast Alabama after Midnight through the early morning hours. The line produced ## tornadoes in southern middle Tennessee, a couple of which continued into northeast Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Raby Road about 0.5 miles before the intersection of Corders Crossroads Road.  The tornado uprooted trees along Raby Road and then intensified to an EF-1 with wind speeds up to 105 mph as it moved southeast. Almost 75% of a roof was removed from a two-person dwelling and the entire roof along with some rafters were torn off of a large storage shed. Additional trees were uprooted and snapped as the tornado moved southeast. The tornado knocked over a power pole and produced structural damage to a house on Warren lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant outbreak of tornadoes occurred in two waves during the evening of the 8th and early morning hours of the 9th. The first wave occurred as multiple supercell thunderstorms developed across southern middle Tennessee and far north central Alabama. These storms moved east during the evening hours, producing ## tornadoes in southern middle Tennessee.||A break in the activity was then followed by a line of thunderstorms that dropped southeast through southern middle Tennessee into north central and northeast Alabama after Midnight through the early morning hours. The line produced ## tornadoes in southern middle Tennessee, a couple of which continued into northeast Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just southeast of Parker Valley Lane. At this location, numerous trees were uprooted or snapped. The tornado continued its path east-southeast across Swan Creek Road, uprooting or snapping additional hardwood trees. The tornado moved across an area of open field before approaching the intersection of along Hopper Road, uprooting or knocking down more trees along its way. The tornado intensified a bit as it approached the intersection of Stevenson Road and Hopper Road before continuing east-southeast along Hopper Road. Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted along its track. As it approached Little Swan Creek, the tornado tore off around 25% of the roof of a home, while continuing to uproot and snap large trees. The tornado turned more to the southeast along Hopper Road and with similar tree damage observed. As the tornado approached the intersection of Gunter Hollow Road, two barn structures were heavily damaged and a small portion (15%) of the roof of a barn was torn off. Additional trees were uprooted or snapped. The tornado weakened significantly after it moved less than a half of a mile east-southeast along Gunter Hollow Road before lifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted along the Tennessee River Valley through September. Abnormally dry and unseasonably warm conditions were present. Pastures across the area provided little to no feed and supplemental feeding of livestock was required. Tropical Depression Helene was able to squash worsening conditions at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) conditions persisted through late month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) conditions expand across portions of Southern Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) conditions expanded into Southern Middle Tennessee as of the 9/10/2024 Drought Monitor issuance, lingering through the end of the month of September. Specifically, Franklin County, Tennessee was added with this week's Drought Monitor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system, coupled with an unstable atmosphere, allowed for scattered thunderstorms. Of these, one isolated supercell produced a couple instances of severe weather, including the first recorded September tornado in East Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Concurrent to a public report of one inch hail, a trained spotter reported a measured 55 mph wind gust along with small to medium broken tree branches. Both events occurred in Bloomingdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted along the Tennessee River Valley through September. Abnormally dry and unseasonably warm conditions were present. Pastures across the area provided little to no feed and supplemental feeding of livestock was required. Tropical Depression Helene was able to squash worsening conditions at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) conditions persisted through late month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system, coupled with an unstable atmosphere, allowed for scattered thunderstorms. Of these, one isolated supercell produced a couple instances of severe weather, including the first recorded September tornado in East Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first began near the intersection of Highway 33 and Jimmie Roberts Road, where branches were snapped and some metal roofing pieces were found in a field. The twister continued along the Clinch River and Alder Road, where numerous tree trunks were snapped, and a few trees were uprooted. The tornado lifted near the intersection of Chestnut Ridge Road and Highway 70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant outbreak of tornadoes occurred in two waves during the evening of the 8th and early morning hours of the 9th. The first wave occurred as multiple supercell thunderstorms developed across southern middle Tennessee and far north central Alabama. These storms moved east during the evening hours, producing ## tornadoes in southern middle Tennessee.||A break in the activity was then followed by a line of thunderstorms that dropped southeast through southern middle Tennessee into north central and northeast Alabama after Midnight through the early morning hours. The line produced ## tornadoes in southern middle Tennessee, a couple of which continued into northeast Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on South Patrick Road near Eastwood Estates causing structural damage to a manufactured home and uprooted numerous trees. It continued to the southeast across Old Quick Road, uprooting and splitting numerous additional trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched southwest of Flintville producing tree damage off of North George Road. The track continued southeast passing to the south and southeast of Flintville with numerous trees snapped or uprooted.  At this point, the width of the tornado was estimated at 300 yards.  The tornado then crossed into Madison County in Alabama.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that moved eastward through Middle Tennessee on September 24, 2024. With plenty of instability and wind shear, these thunderstorms strengthened and became severe through the afternoon and evening. Damaging winds were the primary threat with numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines received. The most significant damage was reported in Ethridge where severe winds caused a tent to collapse, injuring three people. Some hail reports were received as well, but these were mostly less than one inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed a few powerline at the intersection of Highland Road and Obryan Avenue in Ridgetop. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Helene at the end of September 2024 provided significant drought relief in October. At the beginning of October, only about 2 percent of Middle Tennessee was in at least severe drought, compared to about 75 percent of the area at the end of September. Unfortunately, October 2024 proved to be one of the driest Octobers on record, ranking at number 13 going back to 1871. This caused drought conditions to worsen yet again, and by the end of the month, about 11 percent of Middle Tennessee was in at least severe drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from September 2024 improved in October with abnormally dry conditions across Montgomery County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow showers associated with the core of an upper trough passage led to light snow accumulation in the higher elevations of the Smoky Mountains. Wintry weather in the Great Smoky Mountains National Park led to an 18 hour closure of US Highway 441, a critical road made even more important as one of the few roads to western North Carolina in the aftermath of Hurricane Helene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow showers led to a storm total of three inches of snow atop Mt LeConte around 6,000 feet of elevation, as measured at the LeConte Lodge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers led to a light snow at Newfound Gap, reaching 2.6 inches of snow at the mountain pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very small swath of Severe (D2) Drought Conditions returned near the Tennessee River in late August. Soil moisture and crop moisture remained below normal for August. Extreme (D3) Conditions neared the Tennessee River Valley, but enough rainfall from Tropical Storm Francine across West Tennessee prohibited the worsening and spread of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought Conditions returned near the Tennessee River and persisted for the remainder of the month.

Extreme (D3) Conditions neared the Tennessee River Valley, but enough rainfall from Tropical Storm Francine across West Tennessee prohibited the worsening and spread of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought Conditions returned near the Tennessee River and persisted for the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Helene at the end of September 2024 provided significant drought relief in October. At the beginning of October, only about 2 percent of Middle Tennessee was in at least severe drought, compared to about 75 percent of the area at the end of September. Unfortunately, October 2024 proved to be one of the driest Octobers on record, ranking at number 13 going back to 1871. This caused drought conditions to worsen yet again, and by the end of the month, about 11 percent of Middle Tennessee was in at least severe drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite improvement to moderate drought conditions in September 2024, eastern Lawrence County deteriorated to severe drought conditions again in October.

Thought some areas are slightly adjusted thanks to the rainfall received on Halloween night, lingering dry conditions persist across portion of North Central Alabama and portions of Southern Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) conditions continue from September and linger throughout the month of October for Lincoln County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved south through Middle Tennessee during the evening hours of August 17, 2024. These thunderstorms were severe when they first entered the area with severe winds downing trees and causing minor roof damage. While they weakened below severe criteria as they moved into the Nashville area, winds were still strong enough to down a few more trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed which blocked Chester Road at Birch Bark Drive in Fairview. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed in Hermitage, including on Juarez Drive and along Old Hickory Boulevard near Walgreens. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on August 29, 2024. While these were non-severe, they did produce gusty winds. One particular thunderstorm in Williamson County snapped a few trees near College Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were snapped along Arno Road near College Grove. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More summertime thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of August 30, 2024. Gusty winds were the primary threat with these thunderstorms, and one particular storm in Manchester snapped a tree onto a powerline. Elsewhere, a man in Crossville was severely injured after being struck by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning reportedly struck and severely injured a man worked in a tree along Wilson Road in Crossville. The man was air flighted to a Knoxville hospital due to internal burns and a broken vertebra. His injuries were not life threatening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused minor damage in Manchester. At 5th Avenue and Royal Trail, a tree was split onto a power line. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached 105 degrees in a portion of southern middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index reached 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally dry August led to worsening drought conditions, especially across southwest Middle Tennessee. Precipitation totals were below average across the area with Middle Tennessee's climate stations reporting rainfall deficits of around 1.50 inches to nearly 3 inches for the month. For the Nashville area, August 2024 was the 11th driest August on record, and the summer of 2024 was the 17th driest summer on record. By the end of the month, about 40 percent of Middle Tennessee was in at least moderate drought conditions. Of this, about 18 percent was in at least severe drought status with about 8 percent in extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions across southern Lawrence County in July 2024 expanded to include most of the county in August. Far northern portions of Lawrence County deteriorated to extreme drought conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down across the road about 10 feet over the road at this location on Coldwater Creek road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of decaying mesoscale convective systems moved out of southern Missouri and north central Arkansas and then merged and intensified over the Mid-South during the early morning hours of August 17. 2024. Storms fed on a fairly unstable airmass across the region and were aided by about 30 knots of bulk shear. There were several instances of damaging winds with isolated large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown on to a vehicle in Newbern.

Storms fed on a fairly unstable airmass across the region and were aided by about 30 knots of bulk shear. There were several instances of damaging winds with isolated large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were knocked down in the Gift community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level high-pressure system covered the Lower Mississippi Valley at the beginning of August. Persistent moist southwest flow brought high dewpoints into the region. Hot temperatures combined with dewpoints well into the 70s resulted in widespread afternoon heat index values above 110 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices in excess of 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally dry August led to worsening drought conditions, especially across southwest Middle Tennessee. Precipitation totals were below average across the area with Middle Tennessee's climate stations reporting rainfall deficits of around 1.50 inches to nearly 3 inches for the month. For the Nashville area, August 2024 was the 11th driest August on record, and the summer of 2024 was the 17th driest summer on record. By the end of the month, about 40 percent of Middle Tennessee was in at least moderate drought conditions. Of this, about 18 percent was in at least severe drought status with about 8 percent in extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions across Giles County in July 2024 persisted into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions and Extreme (D3) drought conditions continued along and south of Interstate 40 across West Tennessee, during much of January as below-normal rainfall and warmer-than-normal temperatures started the month. Mid to late month a cold snap and heavy snow event brought wetter conditions and below-normal temperatures to Tennessee. Lake and river levels started the month below normal flow, but upstream snowmelt was able to increase flow to near and above normal.  Drought conditions improved toward the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Drought (D3) conditions improved to Severe Drought (D2) conditions by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface heating produced unstable conditions across the Mid-South by the mid-afternoon on August 30, 2024. Numerous thunderstorms developed across north Mississippi and West Tennessee. A few storms were severe with damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave trough rounded the base of a longwave trough through the Mid-South during the afternoon hours. In response to the waves, an upper level disturbance aimed for the Mid-South and crossed during the evening hours. Plentiful shear was available, but surface based instability was lacking to suppress storm development before reaching severe intensity. While storm intensity lacked across Tennessee, hours of training storms followed in the deformation zone behind the disturbance. Intense rain rates and trailing storms caused flash flooding along the Mississippi and Tennessee state lines resulting in road closures, traffic delays, and water into homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several lanes were blocked due to floodwater.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light snow fell across Greene County, with a trained spotter in Greeneville reporting 1.4 inches of snow during the event. The following morning, CoCoRaHS station reported 1 inch of snow and the CO-OP observer reported 1 inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As 2024 came to an end, precipitation totals for about the western half of Middle TN finished near to above normal for the year. As such, December 2024 saw drought improvement across this area. However, precipitation deficits continued to grow across southern Middle TN as well as along the Cumberland Plateau. Crossville's end of year precipitation total was 46.94 inches which was nearly 11 inches below normal for the year. This meant that drought conditions persisted or even worsened in this area. By the end of December, about 31 percent of the area was in at least severe drought with about 12 percent of this area in extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions from November 2024 improved slightly in December with severe to extreme drought conditions across Giles County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system lifted northeast through the lower Mississippi Valley and mid South during the evening and overnight of the 28th. A squall line along a trailing cold front roared northeast into southern middle Tennessee, producing damaging wind in Lincoln County. One narrow swatch of wind damage, with estimated winds up to around 80 mph, was surveyed in southwest Lincoln County near Taft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow swath of intense wind damage occurred from just southwest of Taft north-northeast across Ardmore Highway along Old Railroad Bed Road. The winds uprooted and snapped several trees in this area, and along Henry Thompson and nearby Pepper Roads.  Along Partin Road, a farm outbuilding was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passing through led to showers switching over to snowfall on the backside of the system as strong cold air advection moved through the region. A light snow then resulted across East Tennessee bringing spots of hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across Claiborne County, resulting in 1.5 inches in Tazewell and 1.2 inches south of Arthur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming low pressure system tightened the pressure gradient and increased winds aloft, bringing strong to high wind gusts across the mountains and immediate foothills of East Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As 2024 came to an end, precipitation totals for about the western half of Middle TN finished near to above normal for the year. As such, December 2024 saw drought improvement across this area. However, precipitation deficits continued to grow across southern Middle TN as well as along the Cumberland Plateau. Crossville's end of year precipitation total was 46.94 inches which was nearly 11 inches below normal for the year. This meant that drought conditions persisted or even worsened in this area. By the end of December, about 31 percent of the area was in at least severe drought with about 12 percent of this area in extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from November 2024 worsened in December with extreme drought conditions across western Coffee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level shortwave weather system coupled with very strong upper level winds helped bring a moderate mountain wave event to the East Tennessee mountains, followed by strong to high winds during the midday hours thanks to efficient mixing of high winds to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KTRI Tri-Cities Regional Airport ASOS recorded a 58 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust. An hour prior, wind gusts in the upper 40s were recorded at the airport at the same time reports were received of damage to unknown outdoor objects and smaller tree branches being broken by the gusts. Wind gusts above 40 mph were recorded in multiple counties across the area, including: Washington, Sevier, Knox, Polk, Blount, McMinn, Monroe, Campbell, and Hamilton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving clipper system brought rain and snow to East Tennessee, with the only noteworthy accumulation occurring in the mountains of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lodge caretaker at Mount LeConte in the Smoky Mountains measured 2 inches of snowfall during this light clipper system. Wintry weather in the park closed several park roads for one day, with Highway 441 closed for three days due to snow and ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel shortwave pivoting through an upper trough helped increase the pressure gradient and increase winds aloft, bringing 50 knots over the mountains. A moderate mountain wave event unfolded, peaking at an unusual time of the day: the afternoon hours. Strong wind gusts were observed in the mountains and prone foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOAA ATDD weather station at Camp Creek Elementary School recorded a peak wind gust of 74 mph at 12:30 PM EST. The period of high wind gusts is estimated based off nearby personal weather stations recording strong winds and wind gusts that at times were at high wind criteria.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cove Mountain Fire Tower recorded a peak non-thunderstorm wind gust of 72 mph at 4 PM EST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOAA ATDD weather station at Camp Creek recorded a 62 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper trough combined with a low pressure system undergoing an occlusion resulted in very strong winds crossing the East Tennessee mountains and producing high winds in the mountains and foothills and strong gusty winds across portions of the Tennessee Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For a several hour period, Knoxville McGhee Tyson ASOS recorded wind gusts above 40 mph. The peak wind gust was recorded at 51 mph at 12:49 PM. These winds, combined with saturated soils, led to multiple reports of tree damage across both Blount and Knox counties, with trees falling on powerlines, blocking roads, and a couple onto cars. Neighboring counties also reported strong gusts, with several 40 to 45 mph gusts recorded in Knox County, and a pair of 40 to 45 mph gusts in neighboring Sevier County. Additionally, the AWOS in Oneida recorded a 51 mph gust at 12:15 PM and the TRI ASOS recorded a 51 mph gust at 4:10 PM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Park Service Cove Mountain Fire Tower recorded several hours of high winds, beginning at 3 AM with the last noted observation at 8 AM containing the peak gust of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying MCS/MCV moved through the Mid-South during the overnight/early morning hours of May 22. A slow moving cold front was stretched over the Missouri Bootheel and moisture surged northward allowing for plentiful instability. This instability collided with remnant outflow boundaries from previous convection. Peak diurnal heating hours, moisture, and enough lift caused damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding across West Tennessee.

As a line of thunderstorms moved eastward onto the Cumberland Plateau during the evening of May 23, 2024, they strengthened and became severe. The most significant damage was reported in White County where a microburst with estimated winds of 90 mph uprooted numerous trees. Elsewhere, heavy rain in Clay County caused several roads to close due to high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across Clay County forced the closure of several roadways due to high water. Damages are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought relief in early October proved to be short lived as an abnormally dry fall persisted across Middle Tennessee. Drought conditions only worsened in November 2024. By the end of the month, about 45 percent of the area was in at least severe drought with about 8 percent of this area in extreme to exceptional drought status. November also saw the return of exceptional drought conditions for the first time since early January 2024 with portions of Giles County experiencing the worst drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite improving in October 2024, drought conditions worsened again in November with severe drought conditions developing across all of Warren County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active May pattern continued into May 22, 2024, as additional severe thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee. Damaging winds were the primary threat with this activity with numerous trees, mainly in eastern Middle Tennessee, uprooted or snapped. Flash flooding was also reported in Houston County where water rescues had to be performed at a daycare.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported south of Inglewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on May 26, 2024, and into the 27th. The first round of thunderstorms that moved through the area during the morning of the 26th was the strongest, producing damaging winds and very large hail. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped across Middle Tennessee with the most intense damage occurring in Sumner County and along the northern Cumberland Plateau. Hickman and Williamson counties received very large hail of two to three inches in diameter which caused damage to many vehicles. While the second round of thunderstorms late in the evening on the 26th and in the early morning hours of the 27th were not as intense, they did produce sporadic wind damage in the form of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed a large tree onto a home on the 3200 block of Burr Road in Springfield. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough pushed into the Mid-South during the early morning hours. A very moist and unstable air mass provided plenty of fuel for the trough to initiate convection. The atmosphere harnessed steep mid-level lapse rates, plentiful instability, around 50 kts of effective shear. and decent helicity. This type of atmosphere supported all weather hazards. Recent heavy rainfall events also heightened a flood threat. The severe threat continued into the next day as a line of storms rolled through just after midnight into the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power poles along HWY 641 North of Paris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread severe weather event impacted Middle Tennessee beginning in the early morning hours of May 8, 2024, and continued through the early morning hours of May 9th. During this time frame, numerous rounds of severe weather moved through the area, causing widespread wind damage in nearly every county in Middle Tennessee, hail up to 3 inches in diameter, and four tornadoes. One of these tornadoes in Columbia prompted a Tornado Emergency to be issued. In addition, major flash flooding also occurred with the worst flooding reported across Robertson and Sumner counties. With nearly nine inches of rain, Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for these counties. This is the first time National Weather Service Nashville issued both a Tornado Emergency and a Flash Flood Emergency in the same event. Unfortunately, this event claimed the lives of five individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down about one mile northeast of the Interstate 24 and Stroudville Road intersection in far southwest Robertson County and moved eastward. Several videos of the tornado were taken by residents and storm chasers as the tornado crossed Winters Road and Maxey Road although the only damage was scattered blown down trees. The tornado strengthened as it turned southeast and followed Ridge Road, blowing down dozens of trees and damaging several homes and barns. Three homes had shingle and roofing material loss along with gutter and soffit damage. One home had fourteen trees snapped or uprooted with one falling onto their home. An older 20-by-40 barn on Ridge Road south of Center Drive was picked up and thrown in a northeasterly direction with metal sheeting blown into and wrapped around trees. The tornado took a sharp turn back to the east and continued blowing down trees before it lifted northeast of the Ridge Road and Corlew Road intersection. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on May 26, 2024, and into the 27th. The first round of thunderstorms that moved through the area during the morning of the 26th was the strongest, producing damaging winds and very large hail. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped across Middle Tennessee with the most intense damage occurring in Sumner County and along the northern Cumberland Plateau. Hickman and Williamson counties received very large hail of two to three inches in diameter which caused damage to many vehicles. While the second round of thunderstorms late in the evening on the 26th and in the early morning hours of the 27th were not as intense, they did produce sporadic wind damage in the form of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Woodbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought relief in early October proved to be short lived as an abnormally dry fall persisted across Middle Tennessee. Drought conditions only worsened in November 2024. By the end of the month, about 45 percent of the area was in at least severe drought with about 8 percent of this area in extreme to exceptional drought status. November also saw the return of exceptional drought conditions for the first time since early January 2024 with portions of Giles County experiencing the worst drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from October 2024 worsened in November with exceptional drought conditions developing across southern Giles County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-South was pinched in the warm sector of two frontal boundaries in the early afternoon hours allowing a few strong to severe thunderstorms across Tennessee. A strong cold front aimed to cross the Mid-South later that evening with a primed environment that featured steep lapse rates, plentiful instability, curved hodographs, and plenty of effective shear. Strong upper level divergence and an intensifying low level jet combined with the environment to produce damaging winds and large hail. Convection continued until shortly after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell south of Chesterfield, TN along Highway 114 approximately two miles south of Highway 412.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Helene caps off a several day heavy rainfall event in association with a stalled closed upper low that drew a stream of tropical moisture into the area on the 25th and 26th. The arrival of Helene on the 27th yielded catastrophic flash flooding and river flooding, as well as significant wind damage as wet soils and strong wind gusts led to forest damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of water rescues in Trade were received at 8:30 am EST. By 9:30 am EST, multiple structures had been damaged by flood waters throughout Trade. Damaging flood waters occurred in Mountain City and northward through Laurel Bloomery through the morning. Employees at the Parkdale Plant between Mountain City and Midway were evacuated by helicopter. Antioch Baptist Church near Midway was completely washed away by Roan Creek, to which it was adjacent. Portions of TN 91 from Laurel Bloomery northward to the state line were washed out. Flooding hazards in the county continued into the night. Reports were received from county officials, trained spotters, social media, and broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave pivoting through the mean flow enhanced a typical northwest flow setup for snow and brought accumulating snow to portions of northeastern Tennessee. Orographic lift contributed to heavy snowfall in the foothills and mountains of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A steady snow event led to up to four inches of snow in Johnson County. A trained spotter near Neva at roughly 2400 feet of elevation recorded 4 inches of snow, a public report on Iron Mountain in Butler at 2600 feet of elevation also recorded 4 inches of snow. Additionally, the Mountain City CO-OP observer reported 3.1 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave pivoting through the mean flow enhanced a typical northwest flow setup for snow and brought accumulating snow to portions of northeastern Tennessee. Orographic lift contributed to heavy snowfall in the foothills and mountains of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several CoCoRaHS stations across the county reported 1 inch of snowfall, with the highest report being 1.5 inches of snow outside of Jonesborough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave pivoting through the mean flow enhanced a typical northwest flow setup for snow and brought accumulating snow to portions of northeastern Tennessee. Orographic lift contributed to heavy snowfall in the foothills and mountains of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest observation during the heavy snow event was a trained spotter report of 8 inches of snow measured in the village of Roan Mountain at an elevation of 3800 feet. In addition, the CO-OP observer of Roan Mountain State Park recorded 4 inches of snow, four inches of snow was measured at 2600 feet on Iron Mountain near Butler, and 5 inches of snow was measured at 3900 feet in the Upper Shell Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave pivoting through the mean flow enhanced a typical northwest flow setup for snow and brought accumulating snow to portions of northeastern Tennessee. Orographic lift contributed to heavy snowfall in the foothills and mountains of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light but persistent snowfall event led to snow up to 1 inch being measured in Bristol. Elsewhere, a half inch was recorded in Blountville by both a CoCoRaHS observer and another social media report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through Middle Tennessee late on November 25, 2024, bringing wind gusts of 30 to 45 mph across the area. While these gusts were non-severe, they did cause minor damage to mobile homes in Bethpage and caused a tree to fall in south Nashville. An indirect fatality was attributed to the fallen tree in south Nashville after a reckless driver crashed into the tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with a cold front downed a tree onto Briley Parkway near Currey Road in south Nashville. A woman, who was fleeing from authorities, drove around a police barricade and crashed into the fallen tree. The crash killed the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A humid airmass was in place, south of a slowly approaching cold front. Afternoon convection produced isolated flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave pivoting through the mean flow enhanced a typical northwest flow setup for snow and brought accumulating snow to portions of northeastern Tennessee. Orographic lift contributed to heavy snowfall in the foothills and mountains of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Mount LeConte at an elevation of 6500 feet recorded an event total snowfall of 4.3 inches. Additionally, the remote weather station at Newfound Gap recorded 1.2 inches of snow during this event. This snow event led to the temporary closure of US Highway 441 that bisects the Great Smoky Mountains National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Helene caps off a several day heavy rainfall event in association with a stalled closed upper low that drew a stream of tropical moisture into the area on the 25th and 26th. The arrival of Helene on the 27th yielded catastrophic flash flooding and river flooding, as well as significant wind damage as wet soils and strong wind gusts led to forest damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe prolonged non-thunderstorm wind event from Helene wrecked havoc across Sullivan County, with dispatch noting an early estimate of a thousand trees being felled by winds that were up to an estimated 80 mph, primarily at higher elevations. In addition, reports from the public including trained spotters noted trees fallen on roads, powerlines, and houses, along with damage to city light posts in Blountville, and sign damage in Bristol. County emergency manager reported one vehicle rescue due to a downed tree, and power outages affecting approximately 2,000 residents. Peak wind gust measured at Tri-Cities Regional Airport was 45 mph at 10:49 AM LST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high wind event from Helene felled roughly 100 trees within Panther Creek State Park, leading to multiple trail closures. A few swaths of trees down were observed, with one swath estimated to be over 100 yards long. At 8:10 AM LST, a 52 mph wind gust was measured at Morristown Airport, matching time when the western half of the city lost power, including the Morristown WFO. At 9:05 AM LST, Hamblen County headquarters measured a 45 mph wind gust. Most of Panther Creek Park's wind damage occurred at higher elevations within the park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe and prolonged high wind event from Helene's elevated winds led to catastrophic wind damage to the forests in Unicoi County, primarily at or near ridge top. A video taken by a member of the public showed extreme devastation along Unaka Mountain Wilderness Area and Stamping Ground Ridge near Highway 107. Hundreds to thousands of trees along the top of this mountain range, particularly the east facing slopes, were felled by the high winds, resulting in dozens of miles of mountain trail closures. Additionally two large trees were felled by wind gusts at a campground in the city of Unicoi. A press release with US Forest Service noted that USFS received a $5.25 million relief grant to repair damages within Cherokee National Forest in Tennessee across several Tennessee counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams were reaching the base of bridges in the area and homes in low lying areas were being evacuated at 8:15 am EST. By 9:25 am EST, significant and damaging flooding was widespread throughout the Roan Mountain vicinity. The impacts and threats from flooding would persist through the day and night. These flooding rains overflowed the Doe and Watauga Rivers, which would impact areas in the county downstream later in the day. Damage values for the whole county will be entered in a subsequent entry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Helene caps off a several day heavy rainfall event in association with a stalled closed upper low that drew a stream of tropical moisture into the area on the 25th and 26th. The arrival of Helene on the 27th yielded catastrophic flash flooding and river flooding, as well as significant wind damage as wet soils and strong wind gusts led to forest damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in releases from Watauga Dam that began to flash flood garages and yards of riverside homes along Watauga Drive in Elizabethton around 10:30 am EST. Flash flooding flowing down the Doe River from the Roan Mountain vicinity began to flood homes and roads in the Valley Forge area around 11:25 am EST. Flooding of areas near the Doe River in Elizabethton began around 11:45 am EST when the stage exceeded 9.5 feet. Much of Elizabethton along either side of North Main and South Main Streets flooded as the Doe exceeded 10 feet. Billy Bob Garrison Field had as much as two feet of water. Homes and businesses flooded on North East Street (adjacent to North Main), where vehicles were moved and flipped by flood waters. At 1:50 pm EST, Sycamore Shoals Hospital on the west side of Elizabethton was evacuated. The Doe River and its flash flooding impacts peaked at 1:30 pm EST with a crest of 10.78 feet, and fell below 9.5 feet at 4 pm EST. The Watauga River crested at 13.57 feet at 2:15 pm EST, and flooding continued into the next day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on the Hamblen County side from the massive flood wave on the Nolichucky River near Lowland. The Lowland river gauge was above its previous record crest for nearly 20 hours, cresting at 35.16 feet at 10:15 am EST on the 28th, 9 feet above its previous record. Damaging flooding occurred, but in mostly rural and agricultural areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Helene caps off a several day heavy rainfall event in association with a stalled closed upper low that drew a stream of tropical moisture into the area on the 25th and 26th. The arrival of Helene on the 27th yielded catastrophic flash flooding and river flooding, as well as significant wind damage as wet soils and strong wind gusts led to forest damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on the Cocke County side from the massive flood wave on the Nolichucky River near Lowland. The Lowland river gauge was above its previous record crest for nearly 20 hours, cresting at 35.16 feet at 10:15 am EST on the 28th, 9 feet above its previous record. Damaging flooding occurred, but in mostly rural and agricultural areas. As the river swelled out of its banks in the area where it becomes the border between Greene and Cocke counties, it flooded Goodwater Road around midnight where an elderly couple were swept away in their vehicle trying to escape. All told, Cocke County estimates $24.3 million in total county damages, which are included in the entry for the Pigeon River record flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Helene caps off a several day heavy rainfall event in association with a stalled closed upper low that drew a stream of tropical moisture into the area on the 25th and 26th. The arrival of Helene on the 27th yielded catastrophic flash flooding and river flooding, as well as significant wind damage as wet soils and strong wind gusts led to forest damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The massive flood wave on the Nolichucky River entered the eastern half of Greene County. Nolichucky Dam crested at 66.03 feet during the early morning of the 28th, setting a new record crest by far. Justified concerns that Nolichucky Dam would not withstand the flood wave were fortunately eased as the dam withstood the onslaught, although the power station building was damaged beyond repair. Downstream, the river gauge at Lowland just outside of Greene County shattered its previous record crest by 9 feet, cresting at 35.16 feet at 10:15 am EST on the 28th. Numerous structures were flooded or destroyed. Numerous roadways were inundated and some bridges were destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: TVA increased releases from Douglas Dam for a few days in response to incoming flood waters from the Nolichucky, French Broad, and Pigeon Rivers. Flooding due to backwater issues caused by the high releases included Tuckahoe Creek, impacting Smith School Road and Kodak Road. Flooding also reported along Curtis Road. Some sections of roadways were flooded a foot deep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cove Mountain Fire Tower within the Great Smoky Mountains National Park recorded high wind wind gusts between 3 AM and 7 AM, peaking with a 79 mph observation at the 7 AM observation.

The arrival of Helene on the 27th yielded catastrophic flash flooding and river flooding, as well as significant wind damage as wet soils and strong wind gusts led to forest damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge on the Pigeon River at Newport exceeded the 12 foot major flood stage at 5:30 am EST on the 27th. (This stage is not unusual, and has happened over a dozen times in the 2000s.) However, the gauge remained above major flood stage for 23 hours, cresting at 29.57 feet at 5:07 pm EST on the 27th, more than 6 feet above the previous record crest that stood for over 120 years. As the flood wave moved down from the state line, it destroyed portions of I-40 and a popular rafting venue. A river gauge near Hartford was destroyed, and flooding in Hartford occurred. Structures and agricultural land near Edwina were ruined. Numerous structures in and around the river in the vicinity of Newport were destroyed or damaged. Substantial and damaging flooding occurred in downtown Newport. All damages for Cocke County (which suffered two other river floods elsewhere) will be included in this entry. Rumors during the afternoon of the 27th that Walters Dam had failed were false, but its power station outside of Hartford had to be evacuated due to the excessive river flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Helene caps off a several day heavy rainfall event in association with a stalled closed upper low that drew a stream of tropical moisture into the area on the 25th and 26th. The arrival of Helene on the 27th yielded catastrophic flash flooding and river flooding, as well as significant wind damage as wet soils and strong wind gusts led to forest damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge on the French Broad River at Newport exceeded the 16 foot moderate flood stage at 10:30 am EST on the 27th. At that stage, cabins along the river near Del Rio begin to take water. The river remained above moderate flood stage until 2:00 am EST on the 29th, cresting at 23.34 feet at 9:15 pm EST on the 27th, about a half foot below its record crest set in 1867. Flooding of roads and structures occurred. This river flows through less developed areas, and avoids the city of Newport. All told, Cocke County estimates $24.3 million in total county damages, which are included in the entry for the Pigeon River record flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Helene caps off a several day heavy rainfall event in association with a stalled closed upper low that drew a stream of tropical moisture into the area on the 25th and 26th. The arrival of Helene on the 27th yielded catastrophic flash flooding and river flooding, as well as significant wind damage as wet soils and strong wind gusts led to forest damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along portions of Hwy 19 was reported at 8:00 am EST. Reports from spotters, social media, broadcast media, and county officials increased through the day, including flooded roadways at 8:51 am EST. Flooding of the Nolichucky River at the Jackson Love Hwy bridge area was noted at 9:32 am EST. Flooding of the grounds around Unicoi Hospital was happening around 10:00 am EST, when a berm was overtopped or breached. The hospital structure was taking water by 11:00 am EST, and evacuation by ground had become unsafe. Occupants were taken to the roof, but winds prevented air rescue while rapidly rising and turbulent river flooding prevented water rescue. The hospital was surrounded and inundated by waters at least 7 feet deep by 12:45 pm EST. Flood waters inundated numerous nearby properties and structures including a church, wrecked nearby portions of I-26, and caused significant damage to factories along the river downstream. Hospital occupants were at last rescued by helicopter in the afternoon. Nine known fatalities found in the county; the final three in the list were North Carolina residents, and it is unknown if they were in Unicoi county when they perished or if they were swept in from North Carolina. Most of the deaths were workers swept from a factory parking lot. Flooding impacts continued across the county through the night.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: TVA increased releases from Douglas Dam for a few days in response to incoming flood waters from the Nolichucky, French Broad, and Pigeon Rivers. As a result, downstream from the dam, water encroached into the Deerfield mobile home park, and the entrance was inundated with over two feet of water. A few other homes along the river in the area had taken some water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Helene caps off a several day heavy rainfall event in association with a stalled closed upper low that drew a stream of tropical moisture into the area on the 25th and 26th. The arrival of Helene on the 27th yielded catastrophic flash flooding and river flooding, as well as significant wind damage as wet soils and strong wind gusts led to forest damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding on the Nolichucky River flowed downstream from Unicoi County. This stretch of the river is a gorge that opens into the flood plain of the Bumpus Cove Road section of the town of Embreeville. Water rescue operations had begun by 9:00 am EST in Embreeville. The stream gauge in Embreeville crossed the 16 foot moderate flood stage around 9:07 am EST. Flooding of low spots along TN 107 between Embreeville and Erwin began around 9:30 am EST. By 10:00 am EST water was beginning to threaten Embreeville homes along the river on Bumpus Cove Road as the river reached 18 feet. The river reached the 20 foot major flood stage shortly after 10:30 am EST, and water was entering some of those Bumpus Cove Road homes. The river gauge at this time was destroyed. The river crested at 33.88 feet during the afternoon (as determined later by high water marks), nearly 10 feet above the previous all-time record crest. Practically every home on Bumpus Cove Road in Embreeville was destroyed. The KOA Campground was destroyed, and even its main building at the highest point in the campground near the highway was substantially damaged. The flood waters progressed downstream all day through Washington County, wiping out homes and structures and bridges and roadways and agricultural land along its path. The threats and impacts of the flood continued through the night.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 100 calls were logged by the Knox 911 dispatch for fallen trees during the Helene high wind event. This resulted in numerous trees blocking roads, downing powerlines, and causing thousands of power outages across the county. Several trees were reported down on Warrior Trail as well. A peak wind gust of 49 mph was measured by Knoxville McGhee Tyson Airport at 3:32 PM LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on May 26, 2024, and into the 27th. The first round of thunderstorms that moved through the area during the morning of the 26th was the strongest, producing damaging winds and very large hail. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped across Middle Tennessee with the most intense damage occurring in Sumner County and along the northern Cumberland Plateau. Hickman and Williamson counties received very large hail of two to three inches in diameter which caused damage to many vehicles. While the second round of thunderstorms late in the evening on the 26th and in the early morning hours of the 27th were not as intense, they did produce sporadic wind damage in the form of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported west of Franklin.

Franklin county was hit with winds between 60 and 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down across the road at this location on Old Shelbyville Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Drought Conditions developed in early July thanks to less than normal rainfall for the area. Should drier conditions persist, drought conditions will worsen this Summer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Drought conditions developed for a sizeable portion of North Central Alabama, including portions of Lauderdale, Limestone, Madison, and Jackson Counties in Northern AL. In Southern Middle TN, Lincoln, Moore, and Franklin Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across West Tennessee during the afternoon of February 16, 2024. The front moved into an area with some instability and numerous showers and thunderstorms developed. A few storms contained large hail.

A widespread severe weather event impacted Middle Tennessee beginning in the early morning hours of May 8, 2024, and continued through the early morning hours of May 9th. During this time frame, numerous rounds of severe weather moved through the area, causing widespread wind damage in nearly every county in Middle Tennessee, hail up to 3 inches in diameter, and four tornadoes. One of these tornadoes in Columbia prompted a Tornado Emergency to be issued. In addition, major flash flooding also occurred with the worst flooding reported across Robertson and Sumner counties. With nearly nine inches of rain, Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for these counties. This is the first time National Weather Service Nashville issued both a Tornado Emergency and a Flash Flood Emergency in the same event. Unfortunately, this event claimed the lives of five individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong straight line winds were found by a NWS storm survey team in the Taylorsville area, north of Lebanon in Wilson County. Several trees were snapped and uprooted along Hamilton Chambers Road, and a few outbuildings were damaged as well. The most intense damage was found along Taylorsville Road where estimated 85 mph winds uprooted numerous trees. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of major flash flooding were received across Sumner County, including Gallatin. Impassable roads included Upper Station Creek Road, Liberty Lane, and Long Hollow Pike. Long Hollow Gardens and Long Hollow Golf Course were also reported to be underwater. A house along Long Hollow Gardens was also inundated with flood waters. Life-threatening flash flooding across Sumner County prompted the issuance of a Flash Flood Emergency for the county. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorm tracked eastward across Middle Tennessee on May 6, 2024. With plenty of instability and modest shear, these thunderstorms became strong to severe, especially across eastern Middle Tennessee. Several reports of wind damage and large hail up to two inches in diameter were received. Two tornadoes were also confirmed. One was an EF-0 near Lebanon, and the other was an EF-1 near Smithville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 1.5 to 2 inch hail was reported from a severe thunderstorm near Bell Buckle in Rutherford County.

During this time frame, numerous rounds of severe weather moved through the area, causing widespread wind damage in nearly every county in Middle Tennessee, hail up to 3 inches in diameter, and four tornadoes. One of these tornadoes in Columbia prompted a Tornado Emergency to be issued. In addition, major flash flooding also occurred with the worst flooding reported across Robertson and Sumner counties. With nearly nine inches of rain, Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for these counties. This is the first time National Weather Service Nashville issued both a Tornado Emergency and a Flash Flood Emergency in the same event. Unfortunately, this event claimed the lives of five individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds impacted the Culleoka area in southern Maury county early Thursday morning. A NWS storm survey team found numerous trees blown down in a northwest to southeast direction across a wide area in and around Culleoka. Some of these trees fell onto homes and buildings. Maximum winds were estimated up to 75 mph. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern across Middle Tennessee continued on May 14, 2024, as yet another line of thunderstorms moved across the area. The primary threat with these thunderstorms were damaging winds with several reports of downed trees received across Giles County, Marshall County, Bedford County, and Coffee County. Very slow-moving thunderstorms also produced flash flooding across portions of Stewart County. This activity was also responsible for one EF-0 tornado near Ethridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the Coffee County Highway Department, severe thunderstorm winds downed several trees across eastern portions of the county, including Casey Road and Bell Road. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorm tracked eastward across Middle Tennessee on May 6, 2024. With plenty of instability and modest shear, these thunderstorms became strong to severe, especially across eastern Middle Tennessee. Several reports of wind damage and large hail up to two inches in diameter were received. Two tornadoes were also confirmed. One was an EF-0 near Lebanon, and the other was an EF-1 near Smithville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated half dollar size hail was reported from a severe thunderstorm in Warren County.

During this time frame, numerous rounds of severe weather moved through the area, causing widespread wind damage in nearly every county in Middle Tennessee, hail up to 3 inches in diameter, and four tornadoes. One of these tornadoes in Columbia prompted a Tornado Emergency to be issued. In addition, major flash flooding also occurred with the worst flooding reported across Robertson and Sumner counties. With nearly nine inches of rain, Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for these counties. This is the first time National Weather Service Nashville issued both a Tornado Emergency and a Flash Flood Emergency in the same event. Unfortunately, this event claimed the lives of five individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tSpotter reported a few trees were blown down across Grundy County. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-3 tornado touched down along the Duck River east of Columbia and moved northeast causing tree damage and minor structure damage on Mount Olivet Road. Multiple homes received damage along Cothran Road and Cranford Hollow Road before the tornado moved north of Highway 412. More intense damage began to occur on Old Highway 99 and Blackburn Lane where large sections of roofs were removed and hundreds of trees were snapped or uprooted. The tornado reached its maximum strength along Blackburn Road and Lasea Road where large transmission power trusses were collapsed, a house was destroyed with only small interior walls still standing, and a mobile home was swept away. The tornado continued northeast damaging additional homes before crossing Interstate 65 near mile marker 47. More homes were damaged along Rally Hill Cutoff Road and Kedron Road. The tornado crossed Highway 431 near Kedron Road before lifting in Marshall County. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern across Middle Tennessee continued on May 14, 2024, as yet another line of thunderstorms moved across the area. The primary threat with these thunderstorms were damaging winds with several reports of downed trees received across Giles County, Marshall County, Bedford County, and Coffee County. Very slow-moving thunderstorms also produced flash flooding across portions of Stewart County. This activity was also responsible for one EF-0 tornado near Ethridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down on Paige Road north of Ethridge and moved eastward along E Edan Rd and Marcella Falls Road before lifting near the Giles County line. Damage primarily consisted of numerous trees that were snapped, uprooted, or lost significant branches. A few farm outbuildings were destroyed, and a few houses had damage to the roof or flashing peeled off. Damages are a rough estimate.

Life-threatening flash flooding prompted the issuance of a Flash Flood Emergency for Robertson County. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds impacted the Eagleville area in southwest Rutherford County on Wednesday evening. A NWS storm survey team determined that peak winds up to 90 mph downed several trees and large tree limbs, especially on Highway 99, Taylor Road, and Jackson Ridge Road. Several homes and outbuildings also suffered roof damage. Some of the worst damage was at Lucky Ladd Farms where about 50 trees were snapped. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found straight line wind damage in Giles County, particularly around the Pulaski area. Winds were estimated up to 75 mph. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorm tracked eastward across Middle Tennessee on May 6, 2024. With plenty of instability and modest shear, these thunderstorms became strong to severe, especially across eastern Middle Tennessee. Several reports of wind damage and large hail up to two inches in diameter were received. Two tornadoes were also confirmed. One was an EF-0 near Lebanon, and the other was an EF-1 near Smithville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated quarter size hail was reported from a severe thunderstorm in Lebanon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-2 tornado touched down along Case Road southwest of Prospect where some trees were damaged. As the tornado continued east, a home sustained roof damage, and a tied-down manufactured home was completely removed from its base and destroyed. One person was critically injured in the manufactured home. As the tornado continued east, major tree damage occurred along Bethel Prospect Road, a metal shop building was destroyed, and another tied-down mobile was completely lifted off its base and wrapped around a tree with the remaining debris blown down a hillside. More trees continued to be blown down with roof damage to some homes and outbuildings as the tornado passed across the south edge of the town of Prospect and crossed Veto Road. The tornado continued to snap and uproot hundreds of trees as it moved up and down hills along El Reed Road before crossing the Elk River and dissipating. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-3 tornado touched down along the Duck River east of Columbia and moved northeast causing tree damage and minor structure damage on Mount Olivet Road. Multiple homes received damage along Cothran Road and Cranford Hollow Road before the tornado moved north of Highway 412. More intense damage began to occur on Old Highway 99 and Blackburn Lane where large sections of roofs were removed and hundreds of trees were snapped or uprooted. The tornado reached its maximum strength along Blackburn Road and Lasea Road where large transmission power trusses were collapsed, a house was destroyed with only small interior walls still standing, and a mobile home was swept away. The tornado continued northeast damaging additional homes before crossing Interstate 65 near mile marker 47. More homes were damaged along Rally Hill Cutoff Road and Kedron Road. The tornado crossed Highway 431 near Kedron Road before lifting in Marshall County. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorm tracked eastward across Middle Tennessee on May 6, 2024. With plenty of instability and modest shear, these thunderstorms became strong to severe, especially across eastern Middle Tennessee. Several reports of wind damage and large hail up to two inches in diameter were received. Two tornadoes were also confirmed. One was an EF-0 near Lebanon, and the other was an EF-1 near Smithville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated quarter size hail was reported from a severe thunderstorm east of Smithville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern across Middle Tennessee continued on May 14, 2024, as yet another line of thunderstorms moved across the area. The primary threat with these thunderstorms were damaging winds with several reports of downed trees received across Giles County, Marshall County, Bedford County, and Coffee County. Very slow-moving thunderstorms also produced flash flooding across portions of Stewart County. This activity was also responsible for one EF-0 tornado near Ethridge.

Damaging winds were the primary threat with this activity with numerous trees, mainly in eastern Middle Tennessee, uprooted or snapped. Flash flooding was also reported in Houston County where water rescues had to be performed at a daycare.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed several trees in Celina and caused roofing damage to a few outbuildings. Damages are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down east of Highway 70 in northwest Smithville then moved east paralleling Allen Ferry Road. Numerous trees were broken, snapped, and uprooted along the road, and several farm buildings lost roofing. The tornado then curved southeast and caused the worst damage along Highway 56 north of Smith Road where dozens of trees were snapped and uprooted and a couple homes suffered significant roof, garage, and exterior wall damage. The tornado then lifted east of the highway. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted Middle Tennessee on May 26, 2024, and into the 27th. The first round of thunderstorms that moved through the area during the morning of the 26th was the strongest, producing damaging winds and very large hail. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped across Middle Tennessee with the most intense damage occurring in Sumner County and along the northern Cumberland Plateau. Hickman and Williamson counties received very large hail of two to three inches in diameter which caused damage to many vehicles. While the second round of thunderstorms late in the evening on the 26th and in the early morning hours of the 27th were not as intense, they did produce sporadic wind damage in the form of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds downed several trees across Pickett County. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread severe weather event impacted Middle Tennessee beginning in the early morning hours of May 8, 2024, and continued through the early morning hours of May 9th. During this time frame, numerous rounds of severe weather moved through the area, causing widespread wind damage in nearly every county in Middle Tennessee, hail up to 3 inches in diameter, and four tornadoes. One of these tornadoes in Columbia prompted a Tornado Emergency to be issued. In addition, major flash flooding also occurred with the worst flooding reported across Robertson and Sumner counties. With nearly nine inches of rain, Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for these counties. This is the first time National Weather Service Nashville issued both a Tornado Emergency and a Flash Flood Emergency in the same event. Unfortunately, this event claimed the lives of five individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds resulted in downed trees and powerlines in the Dickson Springs area west of Carthage. Damages are a rough estimate.
